_meta.note: 29 frozen ids (isNew=false) enriched + 39 new (isNew=true). Existing ids byte-stable; new ids carry posesNote (rig|reuse|caption-only).

movements.name: Slow Neck Circles
movements.dose: 40-50 secs; slow half-circles, ~6-8 sec per pass, no holds, no rest needed. No Sweat: 40 secs, smaller range. Slightly Sweaty: 50 secs as a fuller cervical warm-up before upper-body work.
movements.setup: Stand or sit tall, feet hip-width and heavy, shoulders relaxed down, jaw soft.
steps: Let one ear drift toward that shoulder.
Roll the chin slowly down across the chest toward the other shoulder.
Keep it to gentle half-circles at the front; do not crank the head backward.
Reverse direction whenever you like, matching the pace of your breath.
veraCues: Let your ear drift toward your shoulder
Roll slowly, like you have all day
Soften your jaw and keep breathing
Switch direction whenever you like
movements.breath: Breathe slow and continuous; there is no exertion phase, so simply do not hold the breath. A long exhale as the chin lowers helps the neck release.
movements.regression: Seated micro-version: tiny yes-and-no nods and small ear-to-shoulder tilts with no full circles, ideal if standing balance or dizziness is a concern.
movements.progression: Add a 2-3 second pause where the side body feels tightest, breathing into it before continuing — a stretch-hold rather than more speed (range and control, never intensity).

contraindications.flag: neck pain, prior cervical injury, or nerve symptoms (tingling down the arm)
contraindications.why: Full circles and any backward dropping of the head can compress sensitive cervical structures.
contraindications.substitution: Stay with small forward half-arcs and gentle nods only; never take the head into extension.

contraindications.flag: dizziness, vertigo, or low blood pressure on standing
contraindications.why: Moving the head through range can provoke positional dizziness and a fall risk while standing.
contraindications.substitution: Perform fully seated and reduce the range to small, slow tilts.

contraindications.why: Sustained breath-holding raises blood pressure; this move tempts people to hold while concentrating.
contraindications.substitution: Keep breathing audibly and continuously; no breath retention.

movements.intent: Release the neck tension that builds from hours gazing down at a baby or a phone, so the head feels lighter and the rest of the session starts settled.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: neck-rolls

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Full neck CIRCLES (especially full circumduction through extension) can compress the cervical facets and, in a small subset, provoke dizziness/vertigo or aggravate cervical radiculopathy. The source copy and dose already specify SLOW HALF-circles with no full backward roll, which is the safe form. No impact, no BP concern.
vetting.evidenceBasis: General cervical-spine mobility consensus; full-circumduction-through-extension is commonly de-emphasised in physiotherapy practice [evidence: thin on a single named guideline — flag; reasoning stated plainly].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Keep it strictly to slow half-circles (ear-to-shoulder, chin-to-chest arcs) and never a full rearward circumduction. Cue 'if you feel any dizziness, stop and look forward.' Both tiers are non-exertional; correctly not offered as super_sweaty.

movements.name: Shoulder Rolls
movements.dose: 40-50 secs; ~5 sec per slow circle. No Sweat: small lazy circles. Slightly/Super Sweaty: bigger, brisker circles to raise shoulder-girdle temperature before loaded upper-body work. Super Sweaty eligibility depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
movements.setup: Stand or sit tall, arms hanging heavy, feet planted hip-width.
steps: Lift both shoulders up toward the ears.
Draw them back, squeezing gently between the shoulder blades.
Let them melt all the way down.
Keep the arms heavy and passive; reverse direction halfway through.
veraCues: Lift your shoulders right up to your ears
Roll them back and let them melt down
Make the circles big and lazy
Let your arms hang heavy
movements.breath: Easy continuous breathing; inhale as the shoulders lift, exhale as they melt down. No exertion phase to brace against.
movements.regression: Single-shoulder rolls, one side at a time, or a simple shrug-and-release if full circles feel awkward in the chest or ribs.
movements.progression: Add a gentle scapular set at the bottom of each back-roll (a 2-second draw-down of the shoulder blades) to prime postural muscles before strength work.

contraindications.flag: shoulder pain, impingement, or rotator-cuff injury
contraindications.why: Repeated end-range rolling can pinch an already irritated shoulder.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce to pain-free range or do small forward-and-down rolls only, avoiding the high arc.

contraindications.why: Vigorous shoulder-girdle motion can tug the upper abdominal wall; comfort, not range, is the guide early on.
contraindications.substitution: Keep circles small and slow until cleared and comfortable.

movements.intent: Undo the forward, hunched-up shoulder posture from carrying babies, car seats, and bags, reminding the shoulders where home is.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: shoulder-rolls

vetting.contraindicationNotes: The mobility move itself is safe at all tiers. My objection is narrow: it carries a super_sweaty tier. Shoulder rolls cannot generate a vigorous stimulus; the only thing 'briskness' adds is momentum at end range, which is pointless. Approving the super_sweaty label normalises a 'super sweaty warm-up' concept I do not want anchoring the screening gate to a trivial move.
vetting.evidenceBasis: [evidence: thin — flag] Clinical reasoning: scapular ROM has negligible IAP/pelvic-floor consequence; tier is a labelling, not a safety, issue.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Drop the super_sweaty tier; cap at slightly_sweaty. Keep the scapular set progression — it is good postural cueing for this cohort.

movements.name: Standing Hip Circles
movements.dose: 45-55 secs; slow generous circles, ~6 sec each, switching direction at the halfway point. No Sweat: small circles. Super Sweaty: larger, brisker circles as a lower-body dynamic warm-up. Super Sweaty eligibility depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
movements.setup: Stand with feet hip-width, hands resting on the hips, knees soft (slightly bent).
steps: Circle the pelvis slowly and wide, as if stirring a big pot with your hips.
Keep the knees soft and the ribs stacked over the pelvis rather than leaning the upper body.
Make the circles as generous as feels good.
Switch direction halfway through.
veraCues: Hands on hips, circle slow and wide
Make the circles as generous as you like
Keep your knees soft
Switch direction halfway through
movements.breath: Relaxed continuous breathing throughout; no breath-hold. This is a mobility ramp, not an exertion.
movements.regression: Hold a wall or sturdy chair for support and shrink the circles; or perform seated on a chair, circling the pelvis on the seat for those with balance concerns.
movements.progression: Widen the circles and add a small knee bend at the lowest point of each pass to gently load the hips, still slow and controlled.

contraindications.flag: pelvic girdle pain or symphysis pubis discomfort (common postpartum)
contraindications.why: Wide circular pelvic motion can aggravate an irritated pubic joint or sacroiliac joint.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce to small, symmetrical figure-of-eight or gentle forward-back tilts within a pain-free range. [evidence: pelvic-health physiotherapy consensus supports pain-guided, symmetrical pelvic movement for PGP]

contraindications.why: Large lumbar-driven circles can over-mobilise an irritated low back.
contraindications.substitution: Initiate from the hips with soft knees and keep the range small.

contraindications.why: Standing trunk circling shifts the centre of mass and raises fall risk.
contraindications.substitution: Hold support or perform seated.

movements.intent: Bring easy movement back to hips and lower back that stiffen from sitting, feeding, and floor time with little ones.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: hip-circles

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Standing weight-bearing hip circumduction, no load, no impact. The progression adds a small knee bend at the lowest point — keep that minor to avoid an unintended deep loaded squat in a warm-up. Standing balance demand is low; a wall is available.
vetting.evidenceBasis: General dynamic warm-up principle (ACSM).
vetting.modificationsRequired: Cap the progression knee-bend depth (small) so it stays a warm-up, not a loaded squat. Offer optional light support for anyone with balance concerns postpartum.

movements.name: Sunrise Arm Sweeps
movements.dose: 50 secs; one breath per sweep, ~5-6 sec each. Naturally paced by the breath, so it scales by feel rather than tempo. No rest.
movements.setup: Stand tall, feet hip-width and heavy, arms relaxed at the sides, gaze soft and forward.
steps: Inhale and sweep both arms wide and up toward the ceiling.
Exhale and float the arms slowly back down the same wide path.
Let the breath, not a count, set the pace.
Lift the gaze gently on the way up if it feels good, keeping the ribs from flaring.
veraCues: Inhale, sweep your arms wide and up
Exhale, float them slowly back down
Let your breath set the pace
Lift your gaze if it feels good
movements.breath: This move IS the breath: a full slow inhale on the upward sweep, a full slow exhale on the descent. The exhale gently engages the deep core, making it a kind, breath-led warm-up.
movements.regression: Sweep the arms only to shoulder height, or sit tall and sweep — useful if overhead reach pinches the shoulders or balance is uncertain.
movements.progression: Pair with a soft heel-rise on the inhale (rising onto the balls of the feet) for a gentle balance and calf wake-up, lowering on the exhale.

contraindications.flag: shoulder impingement or limited overhead range
contraindications.why: Full overhead reach can pinch an irritated shoulder.
contraindications.substitution: Sweep only to a comfortable height below the shoulder pinch point.

contraindications.why: Repeated arms-overhead with gaze lift can momentarily affect blood pressure and balance.
contraindications.substitution: Keep arms at or below shoulder height and keep the gaze level.

contraindications.why: Reaching overhead increases intra-abdominal pressure and can dome a separated abdominal wall.
contraindications.substitution: Exhale fully as the arms rise and keep the ribs knitted down; lower the reach if doming appears. [evidence: pelvic-health physiotherapy consensus on managing intra-abdominal pressure during overhead movement]

movements.intent: Open the chest and settle a busy mind by pairing big arm sweeps with slow breath — a gentle signal that the next few minutes belong to her.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: arm-sweeps

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Breath-paced arm elevation. The progression pairs a heel-rise (balance + calf) — minor fall risk only. No impact, no BP concern. Overhead reach is unloaded and well tolerated.
vetting.evidenceBasis: General mobility/warm-up reasoning.
vetting.modificationsRequired: For the heel-rise progression, cue light fingertip support available. None otherwise.

movements.name: Standing Side Reach
movements.dose: 45-50 secs total; alternating sides, ~5-6 sec per side, breathing into each reach. The loop alternates sides automatically.
movements.setup: Stand with feet hip-width and firmly planted, arms at the sides, spine tall.
steps: Sweep one arm up and overhead.
Bend gently toward the opposite side, like a tree leaning in a breeze, keeping both feet heavy.
Keep the bend in the side plane — do not collapse forward or twist.
Float back to centre and switch sides whenever ready.
veraCues: Sweep one arm up and over
Bend gently, like a tree in a breeze
Keep both feet planted and heavy
Switch sides whenever you are ready
movements.breath: Inhale to reach the arm up and lengthen; exhale into the side bend, letting the lower ribs on the stretched side soften open. The exhale keeps intra-abdominal pressure managed.
movements.regression: Keep the lifting hand on the hip or reach only to shoulder height and take a smaller bend; or perform seated for balance safety.
movements.progression: Add a slow 2-3 second breath-hold at the end of each reach to turn it into an active stretch, or thread it dynamically between sides at a livelier (still smooth) tempo for the Slightly Sweaty warm-up.

contraindications.why: Deep side bending loads the lumbar spine laterally and can aggravate an irritated low back.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce the bend to a small, pain-free lean and emphasise lengthening up over bending over.

contraindications.why: Side bending and overhead reach raise intra-abdominal pressure on the healing midline.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the reach modest, exhale into the bend, and stop short of any belly doming.

contraindications.why: Overhead reach with a lateral lean shifts the centre of mass.
contraindications.substitution: Perform seated or stand near a wall.

movements.intent: Open the side body and ribs that get squeezed all day from carrying a child on one hip, so breathing feels roomier.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: side-reach

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Standing lateral flexion, unloaded. Safe for diastasis (lateral, not flexion/extension load). The breath-hold progression is a passive stretch hold, fine. No impact.
vetting.evidenceBasis: General mobility reasoning.
vetting.modificationsRequired: None.

movements.name: Cat–Cow
movements.dose: 50 secs; one full wave per breath, ~5-6 sec each. As a warm-up: slow and exploratory. In the main block (Slightly/Super Sweaty): a few more rounds linking strongly to breath. Super Sweaty eligibility depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
movements.setup: Come to tabletop: hands under shoulders, knees under hips, spine long and neutral, gaze down. Pad the knees if needed.
steps: Inhale and let the belly soften toward the floor, lifting the chest and tailbone (cow).
Exhale and round the spine up toward the ceiling, gently tucking the chin and tailbone (cat).
Move at the exact speed of the breath, leading with the pelvis.
Keep the elbows soft and the weight even between the hands and knees.
veraCues: Inhale, let your belly soften toward the floor
Exhale, round your spine up to the ceiling
Tuck your chin gently on the cat
Move at the speed of your breath
movements.breath: Inhale into cow (extension), exhale into cat (flexion). The exhale-driven cat gently draws in the deep core, making this a foundational breath-led core warm-up — never a crunch.
movements.regression: Seated cat-cow on a chair (rounding and arching the spine while seated) for sore wrists or knees, or a standing version with hands on the thighs. [this preserves the spinal wave without any wrist or knee load]
movements.progression: Add small thoracic-focused waves emphasising the upper back, or move a touch more dynamically while keeping it strictly breath-paced; never add load or speed that breaks the breath link.

contraindications.flag: wrist pain or carpal tunnel (common postpartum)
contraindications.why: Tabletop loads the wrists in extension.
contraindications.substitution: Make fists or come onto forearms, or use the seated/standing variant.

contraindications.flag: diastasis recti with doming on the cat phase
contraindications.why: The rounding phase can dome a separated midline if the core is not managed.
contraindications.substitution: Soften the cat, lead the round with a strong exhale, and keep the doming away; reduce range. [evidence: pelvic-health physiotherapy consensus on exhale-led abdominal management with DRA]

contraindications.why: Tabletop weights the kneecaps on the floor.
contraindications.substitution: Double the mat or fold a towel under the knees, or use the seated/standing variant.

movements.intent: Wake the whole spine from tailbone to neck and gently reconnect belly and breath after pregnancy — one of the kindest reconnection moves there is.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: cat-cow

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Quadruped spinal flexion/extension wave. This is gentle, unloaded segmental motion — explicitly endorsed as diastasis-safe reconnection work, NOT a crunch (no trunk flexion against gravity/resistance). The super_sweaty eligibility is only 'a few more rounds, breath-paced' — it is correctly NOT a vigorous cardio driver. The prog rule ('never add load or speed that breaks the breath link') is the right ceiling.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Pelvic health physiotherapy consensus and ACOG postpartum guidance favour quadruped breath-led core reconnection over crunch-type loading.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Enforce the stated prog ceiling: breath-paced only, no load. Wrist-loading is present in quadruped — keep wrist-prep available for wrist-sensitive users.

movements.name: Supported Leg Swings
movements.dose: 40-50 secs per side; ~10-12 controlled swings each side, smooth and rhythmic, no momentum-bouncing at end range. Switch sides at the halfway cue. Super Sweaty eligibility depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
movements.setup: Stand side-on to a wall or sturdy chair, holding it with one hand for support. Stand tall on the supporting leg, the other leg free.
steps: Swing the free leg gently forward and back like a relaxed pendulum, keeping the torso quiet and tall.
Let the range build over the first few swings; stay smooth, never flinging or bouncing at the ends.
Keep the supporting knee soft and the pelvis level.
After the set, switch to the other side.
veraCues: Hold your support and stand tall
Swing the leg easy, like a pendulum
Let the torso stay quiet and steady
Smooth swings, no flinging
movements.breath: Relaxed rhythmic breathing in time with the swing; do not hold the breath. This is a dynamic warm-up, so keep the breath flowing.
movements.regression: Smaller swings, or replace with slow standing marches (lifting one knee at a time with support) for those who find dynamic swinging unsteady.
movements.progression: Add gentle side-to-side swings across the body once forward-back feels controlled, opening and closing the hip — still smooth, still supported.

contraindications.flag: pelvic girdle pain or symphysis pubis discomfort
contraindications.why: Swinging the leg across or wide loads the pubic and sacroiliac joints asymmetrically.
contraindications.substitution: Keep swings small and forward-back only, within a pain-free range; favour supported marches. [evidence: pelvic-health physiotherapy consensus favours symmetrical, pain-guided loading for PGP]

contraindications.why: Single-leg stance with a moving limb is a fall risk.
contraindications.substitution: Use two-hand support or substitute seated marches.

contraindications.why: End-range dynamic hip motion can irritate an unhappy hip joint.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce the arc to a comfortable, mid-range swing.

movements.intent: A brisker, fuller-range dynamic warm-up to prime the hips for moderate and vigorous lower-body work, while a wall or chair keeps it safe and steady.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig: tree-pose — it already provides a one-hand-support single-leg standing base; the swinging leg uses the same hipL/R sweep keyframes as the existing standing loops. Honest reuse avoids a costly new rig.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Dynamic ballistic-tending hip flexion/extension swings. Two concerns: (1) end-range momentum bouncing can over-stress a lax postpartum hip/SI joint and adductors — the source already forbids momentum-bouncing, good; (2) single-leg stance is a balance/fall demand. Wall/chair support is specified. No impact if kept smooth.
vetting.evidenceBasis: General dynamic-warm-up biomechanics; ballistic end-range stretching cautioned in pre-activity warm-up literature [evidence: thin on a single named source — flag].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Mandate the support (wall/chair) as non-optional, not 'a wall or chair keeps it safe' — make it a required cue. Keep swings controlled with NO end-range bounce. Cross-body swing progression should stay within comfortable adductor range.

movements.name: Gentle Standing Torso Twists
movements.dose: 45-55 secs; smooth rhythmic rotations side to side, ~4 sec per full pass. No Sweat: small slow turns. Super Sweaty: fuller, brisker rotations as a spinal warm-up. Super Sweaty eligibility depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
movements.setup: Stand with feet hip-width and planted, knees soft, arms loose and relaxed so they can swing freely.
steps: Turn the torso gently to one side, letting the relaxed arms swing and wrap around the body.
Rotate from the mid-back, keeping the hips relatively quiet and facing mostly forward.
Flow smoothly back through centre to the other side, breathing easily.
Keep it controlled — let the arms be heavy ribbons, not whips.
veraCues: Turn from your middle, hips stay quiet
Let your arms swing easy and heavy
Rotate from the mid-back, not the neck
Smooth and rhythmic, side to side
movements.breath: Easy continuous breathing, roughly one breath per couple of turns; never hold. Keep the core gently responsive rather than braced or bearing down.
movements.regression: Seated twists with hands resting on the thighs, or smaller standing turns with no arm swing for those with balance or back concerns.
movements.progression: Increase the rotation range and add a soft thoracic reach (one arm sweeps a little higher on each turn), keeping the motion smooth and the pelvis stable.

contraindications.why: Rotational momentum loads the abdominal wall obliquely and can stress a healing midline.
contraindications.substitution: Keep rotations small and slow, lead with the exhale, and avoid any whipping momentum until well healed. [evidence: thin — flag] Clinical reasoning: rapid loaded rotation raises shear across the linea alba; early postpartum favours slow, controlled rotation.

contraindications.why: Repeated end-range loaded rotation can aggravate the lumbar spine and discs.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce to small, slow, mid-range turns and rotate primarily through the thoracic spine.

contraindications.why: Rotating the trunk while standing can provoke unsteadiness.
contraindications.substitution: Perform seated.

movements.intent: Wake up rotational spinal mobility — the motion behind reaching into the back seat — and raise core temperature before moderate or vigorous work.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig: seated-twist provides spine/chest y-axis rotation keyframes that map directly onto a standing base; pair that rotation data with the stand base. Honest partial reuse; no full new rig required.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Standing rotational spinal mobility. Rotation itself is diastasis-safe (not flexion). The risk is BRISK/fuller rotations with arm momentum creating an uncontrolled torque on a deconditioned/lax postpartum spine and SI joint. The 'super_sweaty' framing as a 'spinal warm-up' is fine ONLY if it stays a warm-up, not a vigorous twisting driver.
vetting.evidenceBasis: General spinal-mobility reasoning; controlled rotation preferred postpartum (pelvic health physiotherapy consensus on gentle, breath-led rotation).
vetting.modificationsRequired: Cue 'pelvis stays facing forward, turn from the mid-back, let arms follow softly — no whipping.' Keep the pelvis stable as the source states. Brisk should mean fuller range at a smooth tempo, never momentum-driven trunk whipping.

movements.name: Ankle Wake-Ups
movements.dose: 30-45 secs per side; ~8-10 slow point-and-flex reps then a few slow circles each direction. Switch feet at the halfway cue. Super Sweaty eligibility depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
movements.setup: Sit tall on a chair or the floor, or stand holding support. Lift one foot slightly off the ground.
steps: Slowly point the toes away, then flex them back toward the shin.
Repeat several times, moving through the full comfortable range.
Then draw slow circles with the foot, a few each direction.
Switch to the other foot.
veraCues: Point your toes away, then flex them back
Move slow through the whole range
Draw easy circles with your foot
Switch feet when you are ready
movements.breath: Relaxed, natural breathing; this is a low-demand joint-prep move with no exertion phase.
movements.regression: Keep the foot resting on the floor and simply rock heel-to-toe, or reduce to point-and-flex only without circles.
movements.progression: Perform standing with light support, adding slow controlled heel raises and toe raises to warm the calves and shins for brisker work.

contraindications.why: Circling through full range can stress healing ligaments.
contraindications.substitution: Stay with small, pain-free point-and-flex and skip the circles.

contraindications.why: Lifting a foot reduces the base of support.
contraindications.substitution: Perform fully seated.

movements.intent: Prepare the ankles and lower legs for standing strength and balance work, and ease the stiffness that builds from being on the feet all day with a baby.
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback — the avatar has ankleL/R rotation but no toe/foot-detail joints to read clearly at scale, so a small isolated ankle motion would not animate legibly; caption-only is honest and the avatar already degrades gracefully to captions.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Ankle point/flex and circles, optionally standing with support. Negligible risk. Calf/shin warm-up via heel/toe raises is benign. Balance demand low and support is specified.
vetting.evidenceBasis: General warm-up reasoning (ACSM).
vetting.modificationsRequired: None. Keep light support cued for the standing heel/toe-raise progression.

movements.name: Wrist and Hand Warm-Up
movements.dose: 30-40 secs; slow wrist circles both directions, then gentle open-close of the hands and a soft forearm stretch. No holds beyond a comfortable few seconds.
movements.setup: Sit or stand tall, arms out comfortably in front, elbows softly bent.
steps: Circle both wrists slowly, a few times in each direction.
Open the hands wide, then make soft fists, several times.
Gently press one palm to ease the underside of the forearm, then switch — only to a mild stretch.
Shake the hands out softly to finish.
veraCues: Circle your wrists slow, both ways
Open wide, then soft fists
Ease into a gentle forearm stretch
Shake your hands out, nice and loose
movements.breath: Easy natural breathing; no exertion, no breath-hold.
movements.regression: Wrist circles and hand open-close only, skipping the forearm stretch, for sore or sensitive wrists.
movements.progression: Add a light tabletop weight-bearing rock (kneeling, easing weight gently into the palms) to prepare the wrists for tabletop strength work — stopping well short of pain.

contraindications.flag: carpal tunnel syndrome or de Quervain's tenosynovitis (both common postpartum from baby-lifting)
contraindications.why: End-range wrist flexion and thumb-loading can flare these very common postpartum conditions.
contraindications.substitution: Keep to mid-range circles and gentle open-close only; avoid the forearm stretch and any thumb load. [evidence: postpartum prevalence of de Quervain's is well-documented in hand-therapy literature; specific incidence figures not asserted here — evidence: thin on exact rates — flag]

contraindications.why: Loading or circling an injured wrist can worsen it.
contraindications.substitution: Skip and substitute gentle finger open-close only, or omit.

movements.intent: Prepare the wrists and hands for any tabletop or weight-bearing work and relieve the strain that builds from constant baby-lifting and feeding holds.
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback — the rig has no wrist/finger rotational joints, so wrist circles cannot be shown faithfully; caption-only is the honest choice and the avatar degrades gracefully to captions.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Wrist circles, hand open/close, forearm stretch. Specifically valuable because postpartum De Quervain's tenosynovitis ('mother's wrist') is common; this primes wrists before any quadruped/weight-bearing work. The tabletop weight-bearing rock progression must stop short of pain — stated.
vetting.evidenceBasis: General musculoskeletal reasoning; De Quervain's is a recognised postpartum overuse condition [evidence: well recognised clinically; specific protocol thin — flag].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Add an explicit cue that if there is sharp thumb-side wrist pain (a De Quervain's flag), reduce weight-bearing and consider chair/upright alternatives. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Standing Spinal Wave
movements.dose: 45-55 secs; one slow roll-down and roll-up per few breaths, ~10-12 sec per full wave. Unhurried; 3-4 full waves.
movements.setup: Stand with feet hip-width and planted, knees softly bent, arms hanging heavy.
steps: Drop the chin and slowly roll the spine down, vertebra by vertebra, only as far as feels easy — no need to reach the floor.
Let the arms and head hang heavy at the bottom with knees soft.
Roll back up slowly, stacking from the base of the spine upward, head coming up last.
Exhale on the way down, inhale on the way up, moving like a slow wave.
veraCues: Chin drops, roll down bone by bone
Knees soft, let everything hang heavy
Stack back up slowly from the base
Head comes up last, easy does it
movements.breath: Exhale as you roll down, inhale as you stack back up. The controlled exhale gently supports the deep core during the forward fold, keeping pressure managed.
movements.regression: Roll down only to hip or thigh height (hands sliding down the thighs for support), or perform the seated version rounding and unrolling the spine on a chair.
movements.progression: Add a brief soft hang at the bottom with a gentle sway, or link several waves continuously and smoothly as a flowing thoracic warm-up — never adding speed that breaks control.

contraindications.why: A full forward roll-down loads the lumbar spine in flexion, which can aggravate disc-sensitive backs.
contraindications.substitution: Roll down only partway with hands supported on the thighs, or use the seated variant and keep the spine closer to neutral. [evidence: thin — flag] Clinical reasoning: loaded end-range lumbar flexion is commonly limited in acute low-back or disc irritation.

contraindications.why: Forward flexion increases intra-abdominal pressure on the healing midline.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce range, lead with a strong exhale, and keep the belly from doming.

contraindications.flag: dizziness, low blood pressure, or hypertension
contraindications.why: Moving the head below the heart and back up can cause lightheadedness or blood-pressure swings.
contraindications.substitution: Roll up slowly with control, or keep the head above heart level using the partial/seated version.

movements.intent: Mobilise the spine segment by segment and gently release the back of the body — a calming, breath-led ramp that suits the gentle No Sweat tier while still warming a moderate session.
movements.posesNote: new keyframe rig — a segmented roll-down sequencing spine then chest then neck flexion with rising/falling root height has no close existing analogue (forward-fold is a static hang, not a segmental wave). Justification: this is a distinctive, on-brand gentle motion worth one new rig; if rig budget is tight it degrades cleanly to caption-only.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Standing roll-down/roll-up is segmental flexion through to a forward fold and back. Two flags: (1) the bottom position is full standing flexion — keep knees soft and avoid loaded/ballistic bobbing; (2) repeated up-from-fold transitions carry an orthostatic (blood-pressure) drop risk — postpartum women, especially with anaemia or low BP, can feel light-headed.
vetting.evidenceBasis: General spinal mobility reasoning; orthostatic-hypotension caution on repeated fold-to-stand [evidence: mechanism well established; postpartum-specific magnitude thin — flag].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Cue 'rise slowly, let your head come up last' and 'if you feel light-headed, pause.' Keep knees soft at the bottom; no ballistic bobbing in the hang.

movements.name: Side Step-Outs
movements.dose: 45-60 secs; rhythmic low-impact side steps, ~1 step per second, optionally with arm sweeps. Continuous, no rest within the set. Super Sweaty eligibility depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
movements.setup: Stand tall, feet together, knees soft, plenty of clear floor to each side.
steps: Step one foot out to the side and bring the other to meet it, staying low-impact with both feet always in contact with the floor (no jumping).
Step back the other way to return.
Build a steady, marching rhythm side to side.
Add gentle arm sweeps or reaches in time with the steps to raise the heart rate a little more.
veraCues: Step out and together, light and low
Keep it smooth, both feet stay grounded
Add your arms if you want a little more
Find your rhythm, you set the pace
movements.breath: Rhythmic breathing matched to the steps; keep it conversational — you should be able to speak in short sentences. Never hold the breath. [evidence: ACSM talk-test guidance for moderate-intensity self-monitoring]
movements.regression: Slow the tempo to a gentle weight-shift side to side with no arms, or march gently on the spot, for those building cardiovascular tolerance or with pelvic-floor sensitivity.
movements.progression: Increase the tempo and step width and add fuller overhead arm reaches to lift the heart rate further toward the Super Sweaty range — still strictly no jumping and both feet grounded.

contraindications.why: Even low-impact rhythmic loading can provoke leaking or a heaviness sensation if the pelvic floor is still rebuilding.
contraindications.substitution: Slow to a gentle weight-shift, exhale on each step, and reduce intensity; this is exactly the kind of symptom the Super Sweaty screening gate exists to catch. [evidence: pelvic-health physiotherapy consensus and ACOG guidance on progressing impact gradually postpartum]

contraindications.flag: diastasis recti with doming or coning
contraindications.why: Faster movement with arm reaches raises intra-abdominal pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Drop the arms, slow down, and exhale on effort; stop if the belly cones.

contraindications.why: Repeated lateral loading can irritate sensitive knees or hips.
contraindications.substitution: Narrow the step and slow the tempo, or march on the spot.

movements.intent: A genuinely low-impact way to raise the heart rate and warm the whole body for moderate and vigorous sessions, honouring the no-jumping rule while still getting the blood moving.
movements.posesNote: new keyframe rig — lateral stepping with a travelling root and alternating hip abduction is not represented by any existing rig (all current standing loops keep the root centred). Justification: side-to-side travel is the core of the move and cannot be faked by reuse; warrants one new rig, with caption-only as the graceful fallback.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Low-impact lateral step-outs with both feet grounded. Honours the no-jump rule. Heart-rate elevation is appropriate for warming a moderate/vigorous session. The super_sweaty progression (faster, wider, overhead arms) stays grounded.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Low-impact aerobic warm-up is consistent with ACOG low-impact postpartum activity guidance and the app's no-jump rule.
vetting.modificationsRequired: None. Confirm 'both feet grounded, no hop' cue is retained at the faster tempo.

movements.name: Bridge Lifts
movements.dose: No Sweat: 8 reps, 2-3s up / 2s hold / 3s down, rest 45s. Slightly Sweaty: 10-12 reps x 2 sets, 2s/2s hold/2s, rest 30s. Super Sweaty: 12-15 reps x 3 sets or single-leg march at top, slower 3s lower (eccentric), rest 30s (Super Sweaty requires the Super Sweaty screening gate).
movements.setup: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat hip-width and about a hand's length from your bottom, arms resting alongside you, ribs softening down.
steps: Find a long, neutral spine; let the lower back keep its small natural curve.
Exhale and gently draw the deep core in as you press through the heels.
Float the hips up into one smooth ramp from knees to shoulders, no higher.
Pause and breathe at the top without flaring the ribs or clenching the bottom.
Inhale and lower slowly, settling one part of the spine at a time.
veraCues: Exhale, press through your heels
Float your hips into a smooth ramp
Soft squeeze at the top, keep breathing
Lower down slowly, bone by bone
movements.breath: Exhale on the lift (the exertion), inhale on the lower. The exhale-on-effort pattern helps the deep core and pelvic floor co-contract and keeps downward pressure off the floor.
movements.regression: Smaller bridge: lift only a few inches and pause, or place a pillow under the hips for a supported, near-static hold with no lifting.
movements.progression: Single-leg march at the top (extend one leg keeping hips level) or a slow 3-4s eccentric lower; stays hip-driven, never a back arch.

contraindications.flag: lower-back pain or discomfort that increases as hips rise
contraindications.why: Over-lifting turns a glute exercise into a lumbar extension, which can aggravate the lower back.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce range to a shallow lift only to where the back stays comfortable, or substitute supported pelvic-breath to re-find core connection first.

contraindications.flag: knee pain in the bent-knee position
contraindications.why: Foot placement too close or too far can load the knee joint.
contraindications.substitution: Adjust feet until the knee is comfortable, or hold a static supported bridge on a pillow.

contraindications.flag: pelvic-floor symptoms (heaviness, leaking) or visible abdominal doming at the top
contraindications.why: Cueing or breath-holding that raises intra-abdominal pressure can stress a healing pelvic floor or linea alba.
contraindications.substitution: Lower the height, slow the exhale-led lift, and if doming or heaviness persists regress to pelvic-floor-connect breath.

movements.intent: Rebuild posterior-chain strength that supports the lower back and pelvis for everyday lifting and carrying, with zero spinal flexion. Aligns with the diastasis-recti-safe, breath-led core approach in ACOG postpartum exercise guidance and pelvic health physiotherapy consensus, which favour bridges over crunch-type loading.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: bridge

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Supine hip extension, zero spinal flexion — a flagship diastasis-safe move. Single-leg march and slow eccentric progressions are appropriate and gated correctly (super_sweaty needs the screening gate). One nuance: anyone post ~16 weeks pregnant should avoid prolonged supine, but the target audience is postpartum, so supine is fine. Briefly cue not to over-arch the lumbar at the top.
vetting.evidenceBasis: ACOG postpartum exercise guidance and pelvic health physiotherapy consensus favour bridges over crunch loading; load progression per ACSM.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Add cue 'lift from the hips, not by arching the low back' to prevent lumbar hyperextension at the top, especially during single-leg march where pelvis-drop/compensatory arch is common.

movements.name: Bird Dog
movements.dose: No Sweat: 5 reps per side, reach 3s / hold 2s / return 3s, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 8 reps per side x 2 sets, 2s reach / 2s hold / 2s return. Super Sweaty: 10 reps per side x 2-3 sets with a 2-3s hold at full reach, optional knee-hover variant, rest 30s (Super Sweaty requires the Super Sweaty screening gate).
movements.setup: Come to all fours with wrists under shoulders and knees under hips, spine long and level, gaze just past your fingertips so the neck stays long.
steps: Set a long neutral spine and gently firm the deep core to keep the back quiet.
Exhale and slide one arm and the opposite leg long, level with the body, no higher than the back.
Keep the hips level, as if balancing a teacup on your lower back.
Breathe at full reach, then inhale to draw the elbow and knee slowly back under you.
Switch sides and keep the pace unhurried; small wobbles are welcome.
veraCues: Reach your arm and opposite leg long
Hips level, like balancing a teacup
Exhale as you reach out, move slowly
Wobbles are welcome, no rush
movements.breath: Exhale as you reach out (the exertion), inhale as you return. The exhale keeps the deep core engaged without bracing or breath-holding.
movements.regression: Move one limb at a time: extend only the leg with both hands down, or only the arm with both knees down, keeping the trunk perfectly still.
movements.progression: Knee-hover variant (see bird-dog-knee-hover) or add a slow 2-3s hold at full reach; load stays anti-rotation, never spinal flexion or extension.

contraindications.why: Loaded wrist extension can be uncomfortable, especially with postpartum wrist or thumb sensitivity.
contraindications.substitution: Drop to forearms on a cushion, or perform from elbows; if still painful, substitute heel-slides on the back.

contraindications.why: Direct kneecap pressure on a hard floor can pinch.
contraindications.substitution: Pad the knees with a folded blanket or move to a supine alternative such as heel-slides.

contraindications.flag: abdominal doming or back-arching as the limbs extend
contraindications.why: Losing core control lets the back sag and raises pressure through the midline, which a healing linea alba does not tolerate well.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce to single-limb reaches and rebuild the exhale-led connection; regress to pelvic-floor-connect if doming persists.

movements.intent: One of the safest postpartum core rebuilders: trains trunk stability against rotation without any sit-up or plank loading. Bird dog and quadruped work are specifically endorsed in pelvic health physiotherapy consensus and ACOG postpartum guidance as diastasis-recti-safe core re-training.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: bird-dog

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Quadruped anti-rotation/anti-extension — among the safest postpartum core rebuilders, no flexion or plank load. Wrist weight-bearing is the only minor flag (couple with wrist-prep). The hold and knee-hover progressions are anti-rotation, not flexion.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Pelvic health physiotherapy consensus and ACOG postpartum guidance specifically endorse bird-dog / quadruped work as diastasis-recti-safe core re-training.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Cue 'keep the low back flat — no sagging, no arching' to prevent lumbar extension as the limbs extend. Keep wrist-prep available for wrist-sensitive users.

movements.name: Deep Belly Breathing
movements.dose: All tiers: 6-10 slow breath cycles, roughly 4s inhale / 6s exhale, no rest needed. Used as the core-connection anchor in No Sweat and as a wind-down/close in Slightly Sweaty. Not a Super Sweaty driver (it is restorative, not exertional).
movements.setup: Sit tall or lie on your back with knees bent. Rest one hand on your lower ribs and one low on your belly so you can feel the movement, shoulders soft.
steps: Inhale slowly through the nose and let the ribs widen all the way around, front, sides, and back.
Feel the belly and pelvic floor soften and gently expand on the inhale.
Exhale slowly and let the pelvic floor lift, like quietly sipping a smoothie upward.
Keep it light, no squeezing or gripping; the lift is a gentle whisper, not a clench.
Let everything release fully on the next inhale and repeat at an easy pace.
veraCues: Inhale, ribs widen all around
Exhale, lift the pelvic floor like sipping a smoothie
No squeezing or gripping, keep it gentle
Let everything soften on the inhale
movements.breath: This movement IS the breath: inhale to soften and lengthen the pelvic floor, exhale to gently lift. This connection-breath teaches the exhale-on-exertion timing that every other core move borrows.
movements.regression: Drop the active pelvic-floor lift entirely and simply practise 360-degree rib breathing, just noticing the soft rise and fall with no muscle effort.
movements.progression: Add a slightly stronger, still gentle pelvic-floor lift on the exhale and hold the connection for 2-3s, or pair the lift with a small heel slide once connection is reliable.

contraindications.flag: pelvic-floor tension, pain, or difficulty relaxing (overactive pelvic floor)
contraindications.why: Repeated lifting cues can worsen an already over-tight pelvic floor; the priority for these bodies is release, not contraction.
contraindications.substitution: Practise only the softening inhale and the full let-go, omit the active lift, and recommend a pelvic health physiotherapy assessment.

contraindications.why: Any breath-holding raises blood pressure; this is avoidable here.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the breath continuous and never hold; if light-headed, breathe normally and rest.

contraindications.flag: dizziness or light-headedness from slow breathing
contraindications.why: Lengthened breath can occasionally trigger light-headedness.
contraindications.substitution: Return to a natural breath rhythm and shorten the exhale until comfortable.

movements.intent: The foundational re-connection between breath, deep core, and pelvic floor after pregnancy, and the timing template for every loaded core move. Diaphragm-pelvic-floor coordination training is a central pillar of pelvic health physiotherapy consensus for postpartum recovery. [evidence: thin on exact protocol numbers — flag] Clinically, the soften-on-inhale / lift-on-exhale pattern is the standard taught starting point and is presented here as connection practice, not medical treatment.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: pelvic-breath

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Foundational diaphragm–pelvic-floor coordination practice — the keystone of the whole flagship. Restorative, no exertional load. One important honesty guard: 'lift on exhale' must NOT become 'grip/clench' — over-recruitment is a real fault that can worsen a hypertonic pelvic floor and is associated with pain/leaking in some women. The existing 'no squeezing or gripping' cue already handles this.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Diaphragm–pelvic-floor coordination training is central to pelvic health physiotherapy consensus; PFMT is endorsed in ACOG postpartum guidance. [evidence: thin on exact protocol numbers — flag]
vetting.modificationsRequired: Keep 'no squeezing or gripping' and 'let everything soften on the inhale' verbatim. Add a referral line: if pain, heaviness, or leaking, see a pelvic health physiotherapist — framed as connection practice, not treatment.

movements.name: Clamshells
movements.dose: No Sweat: 10 reps per side, open 2s / close 2s, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 12-15 reps per side x 2 sets. Super Sweaty: 15-20 reps per side x 2-3 sets with a 2s hold at the top, or a light resistance band above the knees, rest 30s (Super Sweaty requires the Super Sweaty screening gate).
movements.setup: Lie on your side with hips and shoulders stacked, knees bent toward 90 degrees and drawn slightly forward, head resting on your lower arm or a cushion.
steps: Stack the hips so the top hip points straight at the ceiling, heels glued together.
Keep the deep core gently engaged so the pelvis stays still.
Exhale and open the top knee like a clamshell, only as far as the hips stay stacked.
Pause briefly without rolling the top hip backward.
Inhale and lower the knee with control, keeping the movement small and steady.
veraCues: Heels glued together, hips stacked
Exhale, open your top knee like a shell
No rolling back, keep hips quiet
Small and steady wins here
movements.breath: Exhale as you open the knee (the exertion), inhale to lower. Keeping the breath moving avoids bracing and keeps pressure off the pelvic floor.
movements.regression: Reduce the range to a small opening, or place a pillow between the knees and simply press into it gently with breath, no lifting.
movements.progression: Add a light resistance band above the knees or a 2s hold at the top; the move stays a small, controlled hip rotation, never a big swing.

contraindications.flag: lower-back or sacroiliac pain when the hip opens
contraindications.why: Letting the pelvis rock backward shifts the effort into the lower back and SI joint.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce the range so the pelvis stays perfectly still, or substitute side-lying-leg-lift with a smaller arc.

contraindications.why: The lateral hip can be sensitive to repetitive load, particularly side-lying on a hard surface.
contraindications.substitution: Cushion the supporting hip, reduce reps, or switch to bridge for posterior-chain work instead.

movements.intent: Strengthens the lateral hip stabilisers that steady the pelvis with every step, supporting gait and reducing lower-back load after pregnancy. Lateral hip strengthening is a standard component of pelvic health physiotherapy rehab; this variant carries no abdominal flexion and is fully diastasis-recti-safe.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: clamshell

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Side-lying hip external rotation, no abdominal flexion, no impact. Fully diastasis-safe lateral-hip work. Band and top-hold progressions are benign. Stacked-hip cue prevents lumbar rotation cheat.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Lateral hip strengthening is a standard pelvic health physiotherapy postpartum component; load progression per ACSM.
vetting.modificationsRequired: None. Retain 'hips stacked, no rolling back' cue.

movements.name: Tabletop Kickbacks
movements.dose: No Sweat: 8 reps per side, press 2s / lower 3s, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 10-12 reps per side x 2 sets. Super Sweaty: 12-15 reps per side x 2-3 sets with a slow 3s eccentric or a 2s top hold, rest 30s (Super Sweaty requires the Super Sweaty screening gate).
movements.setup: Start on all fours, wrists under shoulders and knees under hips, spine long and level, deep core gently firm so the back stays quiet.
steps: Set a flat, neutral back and softly engage the deep core.
Exhale and press one sole toward the ceiling, keeping the knee bent.
Lift only to where the back stays flat, never arching the lower back to gain height.
Pause, then inhale and lower the knee back under the hip with control.
Keep the neck long and the hips level throughout, then switch sides.
veraCues: Exhale, press your sole toward the ceiling
Keep your back flat and quiet
Lift with control, lower with control
Neck long, hips level
movements.breath: Exhale on the press up (the exertion), inhale on the lower. The exhale prevents back-arching and keeps the deep core working with the glute.
movements.regression: Reduce the lift height so the back stays effortlessly flat, or perform from forearms to take load off the wrists while keeping the range small.
movements.progression: Add a slow 3s eccentric lower or a 2s hold at the top; height never increases at the cost of a flat back.

contraindications.flag: lower-back pain or arching to lift higher
contraindications.why: Reaching for height converts a glute press into lumbar extension, which stresses the lower back.
contraindications.substitution: Lower the range to keep a flat back, or substitute bridge for glute work without quadruped loading.

contraindications.why: Loaded wrist extension is a common postpartum sensitivity.
contraindications.substitution: Drop to forearms on a cushion; if still painful, switch to bridge or side-lying-leg-lift.

contraindications.why: Loss of core control under the quadruped position raises midline pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce range, re-establish the exhale-led core firmness, and regress to bird-dog single-limb reaches if doming persists.

movements.intent: Builds glute strength that offloads the lower back during daily lifting and carrying, with the back held quiet and no spinal flexion. A standard knee-down glute exercise consistent with the diastasis-recti-safe, low-impact postpartum approach in pelvic health physiotherapy consensus and ACOG guidance.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: kickbacks

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Quadruple hip extension (glute), back held flat — no spinal flexion. The principal risk is lumbar HYPEREXTENSION if the foot drives too high; the source already cues 'back flat and quiet.' Wrist weight-bearing again present.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Standard knee-down glute exercise consistent with diastasis-safe, low-impact postpartum approach (pelvic health physiotherapy consensus; ACOG).
vetting.modificationsRequired: Reinforce 'lift no higher than your hips can stay level — height never beats a flat back' (the prog already says this). Keep wrist-prep available.

movements.name: Heel Slides
movements.dose: No Sweat: 6 slides per side, slide out 3s / return 3s, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 8-10 slides per side x 2 sets, with a brief pause at full extension. Not a Super Sweaty driver: it is a low-load deep-core connection move, used in higher tiers only as a warm-up primer.
movements.setup: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat, lower back in its soft natural curve. Rest a hand low on your belly to feel the deep core working.
steps: Find the pelvic-floor / deep-core connection with one soft breath first.
Exhale and gently draw the deep core in as you slide one heel along the floor.
Extend only as far as you can keep the lower back perfectly still and the belly flat, no doming.
Inhale and slide the heel back to the start with the same control.
Alternate sides, keeping the pelvis quiet and level throughout.
veraCues: Exhale, slide one heel slowly away
Keep your belly flat, no doming or bulging
Stop where your back stays still
Inhale, slide it home with control
movements.breath: Exhale as the heel slides away (the exertion), inhale to return. The exhale-led deep-core engagement is what keeps the midline flat and prevents doming.
movements.regression: Slide the heel only a few inches, or keep the knee bent and just lightly press the deep core on the exhale with no leg movement at all.
movements.progression: Slide to fuller leg extension, then progress toward supported dead-bug (deadbug-supported); range only increases while the belly stays flat.

contraindications.flag: abdominal doming, bulging, or coning along the midline as the leg extends
contraindications.why: This is the primary diastasis-recti warning sign; it means the load has exceeded current core control.
contraindications.substitution: Shorten the slide until the midline stays flat, or regress to pelvic-floor-connect; recommend pelvic health physiotherapy if doming persists at any range.

contraindications.why: A rising lumbar curve shows the deep core has let go and the back is compensating.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce the range and re-engage the exhale-led core, or place a small towel under the lower back for feedback.

movements.intent: An early, low-load way to load the deep core through limb movement without any trunk flexion, a classic next step after connection breathing. Heel slides are a standard progression in postpartum / diastasis-recti rehab taught by pelvic health physiotherapists. [evidence: thin on exact protocol — flag] The clinical principle is graded limb load while maintaining a flat midline; this is connection practice, not medical treatment.
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback — no supine-floor rig exists and bird-dog (quadruped) would misrepresent the position; honest captions with the aria-live cues are safer than a wrong avatar.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Supine low-load deep-core connection via limb movement, midline held flat — explicitly inside the no-crunch envelope. Correctly NOT a super_sweaty driver. The progression toward supported dead-bug is appropriately staged.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Standard postpartum/diastasis rehab progression taught by pelvic health physiotherapists [evidence: thin on exact protocol numbers — flag]; clinical principle is graded limb load while maintaining a flat midline.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Keep the explicit 'belly stays flat — if it domes or cones, slide less far' self-monitoring cue, since doming is the key stop-signal. Frame as connection practice, not treatment (already done).

movements.name: Supported Dead Bug
movements.dose: Slightly Sweaty: 6-8 reps per side, lower 3s / return 3s, rest 30s. Super Sweaty: 8-10 reps per side x 2-3 sets with a slow 3-4s eccentric, opposite arm reaching overhead, rest 30s (Super Sweaty requires the Super Sweaty screening gate). Always feet-supported, never the full unsupported double-leg lower.
movements.setup: Lie on your back. Rest both feet on a low stool, sofa edge, or chair so the hips and knees are bent near 90 degrees and supported, lower back in soft contact with the floor.
steps: Establish the deep-core connection with one soft exhale and feel the back gently settle.
Keeping one foot supported, exhale and slowly slide or lower the opposite heel toward the floor.
Move only as far as the belly stays flat and the lower back stays quiet, no doming, no arching.
Inhale and return the leg to the supported start.
Alternate sides slowly; the supported leg never leaves its surface.
veraCues: Feet supported, back soft and quiet
Exhale, lower one heel slowly
Belly stays flat, no doming or bulging
Inhale, bring it back, then switch
movements.breath: Exhale as the leg lowers (the exertion), inhale to return. The exhale keeps the deep core firing and the midline flat; never hold the breath against the lowering leg.
movements.regression: Reduce the heel-lower distance to a few inches, or drop the leg movement entirely and just press feet into the support on the exhale (this becomes heel-slides territory).
movements.progression: Add the opposite arm reaching slowly overhead with the leg, or slow the eccentric to 4s; the feet stay supported and the belly stays flat — never advance to the unsupported double-leg dead bug, which behaves like a crunch.

contraindications.flag: abdominal doming or coning along the midline
contraindications.why: The contralateral limb load is the point where doming commonly appears; it signals the load has outpaced core control and risks the healing linea alba.
contraindications.substitution: Regress to single-limb heel-slides or pelvic-floor-connect; do not progress until the midline stays flat, and recommend pelvic health physiotherapy assessment.

contraindications.flag: lower-back arching off the floor as the leg lowers
contraindications.why: A rising lumbar curve means the deep core released and the back is taking the load.
contraindications.substitution: Shorten the range, keep feet more supported, and re-engage the exhale-led core.

contraindications.flag: neck or shoulder strain from reaching the arm
contraindications.why: The overhead arm progression can tense the neck if forced.
contraindications.substitution: Keep both arms resting down and move only the legs.

movements.intent: A controlled deep-core loader that trains anti-extension stability without ever flexing the spine, keeping it firmly inside the no-crunch / no-sit-up envelope. Feet-supported dead-bug variants are standard progressions in postpartum and diastasis-recti rehab. [evidence: thin on exact protocol numbers — flag] Clinically the rule is load only as far as the midline stays flat; presented as graded core practice, not medical treatment.
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback — no supine rig exists and the support surface is hard to depict honestly with a reused rig; aria-live cues carry the position safely.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Anti-extension deep-core loader. The critical safety boundary is correctly drawn: feet ALWAYS supported, NEVER the unsupported double-leg lower (which behaves like a crunch and can dome a healing linea alba and overload the pelvic floor). The super_sweaty version (slow eccentric, opposite arm overhead) is acceptable ONLY with intact midline control — hence the screening gate.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Feet-supported dead-bug variants are standard postpartum/diastasis progressions [evidence: thin on exact protocol numbers — flag]; rule is load only as far as the midline stays flat.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Make the doming/coning stop-signal an explicit spoken cue ('if your belly bulges or domes, bring the limbs back in'). Hard-code at the assembler level that the unsupported double-leg version is never selectable. Keep the screening gate on super_sweaty.

movements.name: Bird Dog Knee Hover
movements.dose: Super Sweaty only: 5-8 reps per side x 2-3 sets, hover hold 2-3s, controlled throughout, rest 30-45s. This is a screened progression and requires the Super Sweaty screening gate; it is deliberately not offered below the top tier.
movements.setup: Begin on all fours, wrists under shoulders, knees under hips. Tuck the toes and lift both knees a couple of inches off the floor into a low, quiet hover, spine long and level.
steps: From the knee hover, set a strong neutral spine and firm deep core.
Exhale and reach one arm and the opposite leg long while the other knee stays hovering.
Hold the hips dead level, resisting any twist; this is the anti-rotation challenge.
Breathe at full reach, then inhale and return slowly to the steady hover.
Lower the knees to rest between sides; never let the back sag or the belly dome.
veraCues: Hover the knees low, spine long and strong
Exhale, reach arm and opposite leg
Hips dead level, no twist, no doming
Inhale, return slow, then rest the knees
movements.breath: Exhale on the reach (the exertion), inhale on the return. Continuous breathing under the hover is essential; any breath-hold spikes pressure on the midline and pelvic floor.
movements.regression: Drop to the standard bird-dog with knees down, or keep the knees hovering with no limb reach (an isometric hover hold) until control is solid.
movements.progression: Add a longer 3-4s reach hold or a slow tap of the reaching foot down and back; the move never tips into a full plank or any spinal flexion.

contraindications.flag: any abdominal doming, coning, or midline bulging
contraindications.why: The hover sharply raises core demand; doming is the diastasis-recti red flag and means this progression is too soon.
contraindications.substitution: Regress immediately to knees-down bird-dog or bird-dog single-limb reaches; do not return to the hover until the midline stays flat, and recommend pelvic health physiotherapy.

contraindications.flag: wrist or shoulder pain under the increased load
contraindications.why: The hover shifts more weight forward onto wrists and shoulders.
contraindications.substitution: Return to knees-down bird-dog on a cushion, or substitute bridge.

contraindications.flag: pelvic-floor heaviness, leaking, or symptoms during or after
contraindications.why: Higher intra-abdominal demand can overload a pelvic floor that is not yet ready.
contraindications.substitution: Step back to knees-down work and pelvic-floor-connect breath; this is a key reason the move sits behind the Super Sweaty screening gate.

movements.intent: A vigorous-within-ceiling core progression that keeps the proven diastasis-recti-safe bird-dog mechanics while adding hover load, deliberately avoiding the full plank entirely. Anti-rotation quadruped progressions are an accepted late-stage postpartum core step in pelvic health physiotherapy practice; gating it to Super Sweaty with screening reflects that it is appropriate only once core control is well established.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig bird-dog — same quadruped reach geometry; the knee-hover offset is a caption detail and the avatar reach animation reads honestly.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Quadruped with knees hovering adds significant anti-extension/anti-rotation core demand approaching plank-family load WITHOUT becoming a plank. This is the single most advanced core move in the bank and is appropriately super_sweaty-only with screening. Risk: doming, breath-holding (Valsalva → BP spike), and lumbar sag if control is insufficient. Wrist weight-bearing load is also highest here.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Anti-rotation quadruped progressions are an accepted LATE-stage postpartum core step in pelvic health physiotherapy practice; gating to top tier with screening reflects appropriateness only once core control is well established.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Confirm the screening gate is HARD (not advisory) and that prerequisite competence (clean bird-dog and supported dead-bug without doming) is encouraged before exposure. Add explicit 'keep breathing, do not hold your breath' and 'stop if the belly domes' cues. Ensure wrist-prep is offered. Never allow it to drift toward a full plank (prog already forbids).

movements.name: Side-Lying Leg Lift
movements.dose: No Sweat: 8 reps per side, lift 2s / lower 3s, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 10-12 reps per side x 2 sets. Super Sweaty: 12-15 reps per side x 2-3 sets with a 2s top hold or a light ankle band, rest 30s (Super Sweaty requires the Super Sweaty screening gate).
movements.setup: Lie on your side, hips and shoulders stacked, bottom knee bent for a stable base, top leg long in line with your body, head supported on your lower arm or a cushion.
steps: Stack the hips and gently firm the deep core so the pelvis stays still.
Exhale and lift the top leg to about hip height, leading with the heel.
Keep the toes pointing forward, not up, so the work stays in the outer hip.
Pause briefly without letting the top hip roll forward or back.
Inhale and lower the leg slowly with control, then switch sides.
veraCues: Hips stacked, top leg long
Exhale, lift to about hip height
Toes forward, lead with the heel
Lower slow, keep your waist long
movements.breath: Exhale on the lift (the exertion), inhale on the lower. Steady breathing keeps the move calm and avoids any pressure spike.
movements.regression: Lift only a few inches, or bend the top knee to shorten the lever and lighten the load.
movements.progression: Add a 2s top hold or a light ankle band; the lift never goes above hip height where the waist would collapse.

contraindications.flag: lower-back or sacroiliac discomfort if the leg lifts too high or the hip rolls back
contraindications.why: Lifting past hip height or rolling the pelvis recruits the back instead of the hip.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the lift low and the hips stacked, or substitute clamshell for a smaller-range hip option.

contraindications.why: Side-lying on a hard surface can compress the underside hip and shoulder.
contraindications.substitution: Add padding under the hip and head, reduce reps, or switch to bridge.

movements.intent: Builds lateral hip and pelvic stability through a longer lever than clamshell, supporting gait and easing lower-back load, with no abdominal flexion at all. Lateral hip strengthening is a routine part of pelvic health physiotherapy postpartum programming and is fully diastasis-recti-safe.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig clamshell — both are side-lying with stacked hips; the extended top leg vs bent knee is a caption distinction the side-lying avatar pose supports honestly.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Side-lying hip abduction, longer lever than clamshell, no abdominal flexion, no impact. Diastasis-safe. The 'never above hip height where the waist collapses' rule prevents lumbar side-bend cheat and QL overload.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Lateral hip strengthening is routine in pelvic health physiotherapy postpartum programming; load per ACSM.
vetting.modificationsRequired: None. Retain the hip-height cap and the ankle-band/top-hold progressions as written.

movements.name: Wall Incline Plank
movements.dose: No Sweat: 2-3 holds of 15-20s at a high wall lean, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 3 holds of 20-30s, or to a lower counter/sofa-back incline. Super Sweaty: 3-4 holds of 30-40s at a lower incline, optional slow shoulder taps, rest 30s (Super Sweaty requires the Super Sweaty screening gate). This is an INCLINE hold against a raised surface, never a floor plank.
movements.setup: Stand facing a wall, arms length away. Place your palms flat on the wall at chest height, shoulder-width, and step the feet back until your body is one long gentle incline. The higher the hands, the easier.
steps: Set the hands on the wall and walk the feet back to a comfortable lean.
Make one long line from heels to head, ribs softening down, deep core gently firm.
Exhale and lightly draw the pelvic floor and deep core up to hold the line.
Breathe steadily through the hold, keeping the belly flat with no doming, hips neither sagging nor piking.
To finish, walk the feet back in and step away from the wall.
veraCues: Hands on the wall, one long line
Exhale, gently draw the deep core up
Belly flat, no doming, keep breathing
Higher hands make it kinder
movements.breath: Breathe continuously and steadily through the hold; gently exhale and lift the deep core as you settle in. Never hold the breath: a breath-hold against an isometric raises pressure on the midline and pelvic floor.
movements.regression: Stand more upright with hands higher on the wall (closer to vertical is easiest), or shorten the hold to 10s.
movements.progression: Lower the incline to a sturdy counter or sofa back, lengthen the hold, or add slow single-hand shoulder taps; never lower to a floor plank, which exceeds the postpartum core ceiling.

contraindications.flag: abdominal doming, coning, or midline bulging during the hold
contraindications.why: Even an incline can over-challenge a healing linea alba; doming is the diastasis-recti red flag.
contraindications.substitution: Raise the hands higher (more upright) to reduce load, shorten the hold, or regress to bird-dog; recommend pelvic health physiotherapy if doming persists upright.

contraindications.flag: wrist pain in the loaded position
contraindications.why: Weight-bearing wrist extension can be uncomfortable postpartum.
contraindications.substitution: Place forearms on the raised surface instead of palms, or substitute bird-dog.

contraindications.why: The incline loads the shoulders, which may be sensitive from carrying.
contraindications.substitution: Move to a higher, more upright incline to lighten shoulder load, or substitute bird-dog.

contraindications.why: Sustained intra-abdominal demand can overload the pelvic floor.
contraindications.substitution: Raise the incline and shorten the hold; this symptom is a key reason the lower-incline version sits behind the Super Sweaty screening gate.

movements.intent: Delivers a graded anti-extension core hold while explicitly avoiding the floor plank that the safety envelope forbids; the incline is fully adjustable so the same movement serves every tier. Incline / wall planks are the standard pelvic-floor-and-diastasis-safe entry to plank-family loading in postpartum physiotherapy. [evidence: thin on exact incline angles — flag] The clinical principle is raise the surface until the midline stays flat; this is graded loading guidance, not medical advice.
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback — no standing-incline-against-surface rig exists; building one is costly and a reused floor or quadruped rig would dangerously imply a floor plank, so honest captions plus aria-live cues are the safe choice.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: INCLINE anti-extension hold against a raised surface — the correct, explicit substitute for the forbidden floor plank. Load is dialled by incline angle, which is the right adjustable lever. Risks: isometric holds invite breath-holding (Valsalva → BP rise, a real flag if hypertensive), and at lower inclines the move approaches true plank load and could dome the abdomen.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Incline/wall planks are the standard pelvic-floor-and-diastasis-safe entry to plank-family loading in postpartum physiotherapy [evidence: thin on exact incline angles — flag]; principle is raise the surface until the midline stays flat.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Hard-code that it NEVER lowers to a floor plank (prog states this — enforce at assembler). Add explicit 'breathe throughout, do not hold your breath' cue (Valsalva/BP). Add doming self-check: 'if your belly bulges, raise back up to a higher surface.' Hypertension flag in the scaffolded screening should cap the lowest incline.

movements.name: Pelvic Floor Connect
movements.dose: All eligible tiers: 6-10 cycles, inhale-soften ~4s / exhale-lift-and-hold 2-3s / full release. No rest needed. Used as a connection primer in warm-up and a settling close; it is restorative, so it is not a Super Sweaty driver.
movements.setup: Sit tall on a chair or the floor, or lie on your back with knees bent. Soften the shoulders, unclench the jaw and bottom, and rest your hands wherever is comfortable.
steps: Inhale slowly and let the whole pelvic floor soften and gently widen, like a flower opening.
On the exhale, gently lift and draw the pelvic floor up and in, front to back, a quiet 'sipping up' feeling.
Hold that gentle lift for 2-3 seconds while you keep breathing lightly.
Fully let go on the next inhale, the release is as important as the lift.
Keep the bottom, thighs, and jaw relaxed so the work stays deep and specific.
veraCues: Inhale, let the pelvic floor soften open
Exhale, gently lift and sip upward
Hold light for a moment, keep breathing
Let it fully release, jaw and bottom soft
movements.breath: Exhale to lift the pelvic floor (the exertion), inhale to release. The full release on the inhale matters as much as the lift; this teaches both contraction and relaxation, never a constant clench.
movements.regression: Drop the hold and just gently lift on the exhale and immediately release, or omit the active lift entirely and practise softening the pelvic floor on each inhale (release-only).
movements.progression: Add a short series of quick gentle lifts after the long hold (endurance plus quick-response), keeping every lift soft and every release complete.

contraindications.flag: pelvic-floor tension, pain, or trouble relaxing (overactive pelvic floor)
contraindications.why: Strengthening cues can worsen an over-tight pelvic floor; these bodies need release work first, and self-directed lifting can entrench the problem.
contraindications.substitution: Practise only the release-only version (soften on the inhale, no active lift) and recommend assessment by a pelvic health physiotherapist.

contraindications.flag: uncertainty whether you are lifting correctly, or ongoing leaking / heaviness
contraindications.why: A meaningful share of people contract incorrectly without feedback, and symptoms warrant individual assessment.
contraindications.substitution: Keep practice gentle and brief and seek a pelvic health physiotherapy assessment for hands-on feedback rather than self-progressing.

contraindications.why: Holding the breath during the lift raises pressure and can cause light-headedness, the opposite of the intended effect.
contraindications.substitution: Keep breathing lightly through the hold; shorten the hold if needed.

movements.intent: A focused pelvic-floor connection practice that trains both the lift AND the equally important release, the cornerstone skill the postpartum flagship is built around. Pelvic-floor muscle training is endorsed in ACOG postpartum guidance and is a central pelvic health physiotherapy intervention. [evidence: thin on exact rep protocols and on what share contract incorrectly — flag] Because correct technique and symptom screening genuinely benefit from hands-on assessment, this is framed as gentle connection practice with a clear referral, not medical treatment.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig pelvic-breath — same seated/supine breath-led posture; the avatar's stillness reads honestly and the distinction from pelvic-breath is carried in the cues, so no new rig is justified.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Restorative connection practice (lift AND release) — non-exertional, correctly not a super_sweaty driver. The single genuine clinical caveat: a subset of postpartum women have an OVERACTIVE/non-relaxing pelvic floor for whom repeated lifts without full release can worsen symptoms; the move's emphasis on the equally-important RELEASE plus a referral is the right mitigation. From a sports-med/joint lens there is no risk.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Pelvic-floor muscle training endorsed in ACOG postpartum guidance; central pelvic health physiotherapy intervention [evidence: thin on exact rep protocols and on how many contract incorrectly — flag].
vetting.modificationsRequired: None to the movement; keep the explicit referral language and the equal weighting of release. Ensure copy never frames it as treatment for prolapse/incontinence — connection practice only (already done).

movements.name: Chair Pose
movements.dose: No Sweat: 1 hold 20-30s, 1 set, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 2 holds 30-40s, tempo steady, rest 25s. Super Sweaty (screened): 3 holds 40-45s with a slow 2s pulse at the bottom, rest 20s.
movements.setup: Stand tall, feet hip-width, weight settled into both heels.
steps: Inhale and lengthen up through the crown.
Exhale, sink your hips back and down as if reaching for a chair behind you.
Keep your weight in your heels so you could lift your toes.
Reach your arms forward and up to shoulder height or wherever feels easy.
Hold and breathe, then rise on an exhale and rest.
veraCues: Sink your hips back like sitting down
Keep your weight in your heels
Reach your arms forward and up
Keep breathing, you are doing it
movements.breath: Exhale as you sit back and as you press up to stand; never hold your breath under the load.
movements.regression: Half-depth chair, hands on thighs, or sit lightly onto an actual chair seat and hover.
movements.progression: Lower a few degrees and add a slow 2s pulse at the bottom, arms overhead (Super Sweaty only).

contraindications.why: Sustained knee flexion under load can aggravate patellofemoral pain.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce depth, keep shins more vertical, or substitute sit-to-stand from a chair.

contraindications.why: Reaching arms overhead can drive a back arch and load the lumbar spine.
contraindications.substitution: Keep arms at shoulder height or hands on thighs and ribs stacked over hips.

contraindications.why: Isometric holds with arms overhead can spike blood pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Shorten the hold, lower the arms, and keep breathing freely rather than straining.

contraindications.why: Breath-holding during the hold raises intra-abdominal pressure and can strain the floor or midline.
contraindications.substitution: Exhale on effort, keep depth modest, and stop if you feel doming or downward pressure.

movements.intent: Build everyday leg and posture strength for the squatting and lifting of daily life, held still while you breathe. [evidence: ACSM resistance-training and isometric exertion guidance support submaximal holds for general strength; pair with ACOG postpartum return-to-activity progression.]
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: chair-pose

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Isometric squat-hold with arms overhead. Two flags: (1) sustained isometric holds raise blood pressure more than dynamic work — relevant for a hypertension flag; (2) overhead arm position adds to the BP/shoulder demand. No impact, knee load is moderate and self-limited.
vetting.evidenceBasis: ACSM resistance-training/isometric exertion guidance supports submaximal holds for general strength; pair with ACOG postpartum return-to-activity progression.
