vetting.modificationsRequired: Add 'keep breathing steadily — do not hold your breath' cue (counters Valsalva/BP rise during the hold). In the scaffolded screening, isometric holds and overhead arms should be tempered for a hypertension flag. Offer 'arms forward instead of overhead' regression.

movements.name: Warrior II
movements.dose: No Sweat: 1 hold per side 20-30s, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 2 holds per side 30-40s, rest 25s. Super Sweaty (screened): hold 40s per side with a slow sink-and-rise pulse, rest 20s.
movements.setup: Step your feet wide, turn your front toes forward and your back foot slightly in.
steps: Bend your front knee toward a right angle, tracking it over your ankle.
Press the outer edge of your back foot down for a steady base.
Stretch your arms wide, front and back, at shoulder height.
Stack your shoulders over your hips and gaze softly past your front hand.
Hold and breathe, then straighten the front leg to rest before switching sides.
veraCues: Bend your front knee over your ankle
Stretch your arms wide in both directions
Gaze softly over your front hand
Stand proud and keep breathing
movements.breath: Breathe steadily throughout; let an easy exhale help you sink a little deeper.
movements.regression: Narrower stance and less knee bend, or rest a hand on a chair back for balance.
movements.progression: Deepen the front knee and add a slow sink-and-rise pulse while keeping the torso upright (Super Sweaty only).

contraindications.why: A front knee that drifts past the toes or caves inward loads the joint unevenly.
contraindications.substitution: Shorten the stance, reduce the bend, and actively track the knee over the second toe.

contraindications.why: A wide static stance can tempt the lower back to arch and overwork.
contraindications.substitution: Lightly draw the lower ribs down and keep the pelvis neutral; reduce hold time.

contraindications.why: Arms held wide at shoulder height for long isometrics can elevate blood pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Lower the arms to hips and shorten holds while breathing freely.

contraindications.why: Bracing or breath-holding in the hold raises downward pressure on the floor.
contraindications.substitution: Keep breathing, exhale on the deepest point, and stop if you feel heaviness below.

movements.intent: A wide, grounded stance that strengthens the legs and opens the hips and chest, reminding the body of its own steadiness. [evidence: general strength benefit per ACSM resistance-training guidance; postpartum suitability per ACOG graded return to activity.]
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: warrior2

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Wide-stance isometric leg/hip strengthener. Same isometric-BP caveat as chair-pose but lower (arms horizontal, not overhead). Front-knee tracking over ankle must be cued to protect the knee. No impact.
vetting.evidenceBasis: General strength benefit per ACSM resistance-training guidance; postpartum suitability per ACOG graded return to activity.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Retain 'front knee over ankle, not past the toes' and add 'breathe steadily through the hold.' Sink-and-rise pulse (super_sweaty) is fine and reduces the static-BP load.

movements.name: Invisible Chair Squats
movements.dose: No Sweat: 6-8 slow reps, tempo 3s down 2s up, 1 set, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 10-12 reps x 2 sets, rest 25s. Super Sweaty (screened): 12-15 reps x 3 sets with a 2s pause at the bottom, rest 20s.
movements.setup: Stand with feet hip- to shoulder-width, toes slightly out, weight in your heels.
steps: Inhale and sit your hips back toward an imaginary chair.
Keep your chest lifted and your knees tracking over your toes.
Lower as far as feels controlled, no deeper than comfortable.
Exhale and press through your heels to stand tall.
Move slowly, especially on the way down.
veraCues: Inhale, sit back toward that invisible chair
Keep your chest proud and lifted
Exhale, press through your heels to stand
Go slow on the way down
movements.breath: Inhale on the way down, exhale as you press up; the exhale-on-effort protects your core and floor.
movements.regression: Squat to a chair and lightly touch the seat, or reduce depth and hold a counter for support.
movements.progression: Add reps, slow the lowering to 4s, or pause 2s at the bottom (Super Sweaty only); no jumping.

contraindications.why: Deep or fast squats can overload the kneecap, especially with knees caving in.
contraindications.substitution: Limit depth to pain-free range and keep knees tracking over the toes; squat to a chair.

contraindications.why: Letting the chest drop forward shifts load to the lumbar spine.
contraindications.substitution: Sit back more, keep the chest lifted, and reduce depth; box squat to a chair.

contraindications.why: Holding the breath or rushing the rep increases downward pressure and can cause doming or leaking.
contraindications.substitution: Exhale on the stand, keep depth modest, and stop if you feel pressure, doming, or leakage.

contraindications.why: Repeated straining with held breath can raise blood pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Keep breathing rhythmically and avoid grinding maximal reps.

movements.intent: The most useful strength there is, rehearsing the real-life lifting of laundry baskets and small humans. [evidence: squatting is a foundational functional pattern; load progression per ACSM, and postpartum graded loading aligns with ACOG and pelvic-health physiotherapy consensus on breath-led core engagement.]
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: squats

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Bodyweight squat — foundational functional pattern, no impact (no jumping, confirmed). Knee load is self-limited and the heels-down/chest-proud cues protect knees and back. The bottom-pause super_sweaty variant increases time-under-tension safely. Breath coordination matters to protect the pelvic floor (exhale on exertion).
vetting.evidenceBasis: Squat is a foundational functional pattern; load progression per ACSM; postpartum graded loading aligns with ACOG and pelvic-health physiotherapy consensus on breath-led core engagement.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Add 'exhale as you stand' cue so intra-abdominal pressure is managed (protects pelvic floor/linea alba) — partially present. Offer a depth regression (squat to a chair) for knee-sensitive users. No jumping (confirmed).

movements.name: Baby Cobra
movements.dose: No Sweat: 5-6 gentle lifts holding 2-3s each, 1 set, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 8-10 lifts holding 3s each x 2 sets, rest 25s.
movements.setup: Lie face down, hands under your shoulders, forehead resting, legs long.
steps: Lengthen the back of your neck and draw your shoulders down away from your ears.
Press lightly through your hands without pushing yourself up by force.
Inhale and lift your chest just a little using your upper back.
Keep the lift low and easy, hips and pubic bone staying on the floor.
Exhale and lower with control, resting the forehead down.
veraCues: Press lightly through your hands
Inhale, lift your chest just a little
Slide your shoulders away from your ears
Keep it gentle, low is lovely
movements.breath: Inhale to lift, exhale to lower; keep the breath flowing so the belly is not braced hard.
movements.regression: Sphinx on the forearms with an even smaller lift, or simply a chest-opening shoulder squeeze.
movements.progression: Hold the top for an extra breath or add a light back-of-arm reach; keep the lift low and never strain.

contraindications.why: A high back-bend compresses the lumbar spine and can aggravate back pain.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the lift very low (sphinx height) or skip the extension and do a seated chest-opener.

contraindications.why: Loading the wrists in extension can be uncomfortable.
contraindications.substitution: Move to forearms (sphinx) so the wrists are unloaded.

contraindications.why: Back extension stretches the abdominal wall and can stress a healing midline if pushed too far.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the lift minimal, exhale on the rise, and stop if you see midline doming; favor sphinx height.

movements.intent: Counter the hours of curling forward to feed and carry by gently strengthening the upper back and opening the chest. [evidence: thoracic extension counters habitual flexed posture (general musculoskeletal reasoning); kept low to respect ACOG postpartum guidance and diastasis-recti caution against aggressive abdominal stretch.]
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: baby-cobra

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Prone gentle thoracic extension. This is a back-EXTENSION over a stretched abdominal wall — in early/unhealed diastasis an aggressive prone extension can stretch and stress the linea alba. The source correctly keeps it LOW and gentle and excludes it from super_sweaty. Lumbar hyperextension and any prone position discomfort (breast tenderness postpartum) are minor flags.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Thoracic extension counters habitual flexed posture (general musculoskeletal reasoning); kept low to respect ACOG postpartum guidance and diastasis caution against aggressive abdominal stretch.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Keep the lift explicitly LOW (chest just off the floor, ribs stay down) and cue 'lead with the upper back, keep the lift small.' Add a note that very early postpartum or symptomatic diastasis users keep it minimal or skip. Confirm it never becomes upward-dog/full cobra.

movements.name: Tree Pose
movements.dose: No Sweat: 1 hold per side 15-25s, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 2 holds per side 25-35s, rest 25s.
movements.setup: Stand tall, weight settling evenly into one foot, a wall or chair within reach.
steps: Find a steady spot to gaze at to help your balance.
Shift your weight into your standing leg and root the foot down.
Rest the other foot on your ankle, calf, or inner thigh, never on the knee.
Bring your hands together at your heart or rest one hand on support.
Hold and breathe, then switch sides.
veraCues: Rest your foot on your calf
Press your hands together at your heart
Find one spot to gaze at
Wobbling is just balance practice
movements.breath: Breathe slow and even; steady breath steadies balance.
movements.regression: Keep the toes of the lifted foot lightly on the floor like a kickstand, or hold a chair.
movements.progression: Float the foot to the inner thigh and lift the hands overhead once balance is solid.

contraindications.why: Placing the lifted foot directly on the standing knee presses the joint sideways.
contraindications.substitution: Place the foot above or below the knee, never on it.

contraindications.why: Reaching the arms overhead can arch the back.
contraindications.substitution: Keep hands at the heart and ribs stacked over hips.

movements.intent: Strengthen the feet, ankles, and hips while giving the mind one quiet thing to hold, with wobbling reframed as balance practice rather than failure. [evidence: single-leg balance training supports lower-limb stability (general motor-control reasoning); appropriate at all postpartum stages.]
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: tree-pose

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Single-leg balance hold. Only risk is a fall; reframing wobble as practice is good, but postpartum balance/relaxin-related laxity can be reduced. No impact, no BP, no core-pressure issue.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Single-leg balance training supports lower-limb stability (general motor-control reasoning); appropriate at all postpartum stages.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Add 'a wall or chair nearby is smart' cue and 'foot on the calf, not the side of the knee' (protects the standing knee). Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Goddess Hold
movements.dose: No Sweat: 1 hold 20-30s, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 2 holds 30-40s, rest 25s. Super Sweaty (screened): 3 holds 40s with slow pulses, or 12-15 small pulse reps x 2-3 sets, rest 20s.
movements.setup: Step wide, turn your toes out comfortably, hands in a cactus shape or at your heart.
steps: Bend your knees and sink your hips down, tracking knees over your toes.
Keep your chest proud and your tailbone heavy, not tucked or arched.
Hold steady, or add small low pulses for more.
Press through your heels to rise when you are ready.
Breathe steady, strong, and still.
veraCues: Toes out, knees track over your toes
Sink an inch lower, you've got this
Cactus arms, chest proud
Breathe steady, strong and still
movements.breath: Breathe steadily in the hold; if you pulse, exhale on each press up.
movements.regression: Narrower stance, less depth, or hold a chair back for balance.
movements.progression: Sink lower and add slow pulses or longer holds while keeping the chest tall (Super Sweaty only).

contraindications.why: A wide deep hold can stress the knees if they cave inward.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce depth and width and keep knees pressing gently out over the toes.

contraindications.why: Cactus arms can encourage a back arch during a long hold.
contraindications.substitution: Soften the ribs down, lower the arms to the heart, and shorten the hold.

contraindications.why: Long isometric holds, especially with arms up, can raise blood pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Shorten holds, lower arms, and keep breathing freely.

contraindications.why: Breath-holding in a wide squat raises downward pressure on the pelvic floor.
contraindications.substitution: Keep breathing, exhale on effort, and stop if you feel heaviness or doming.

movements.intent: A proud, wide-legged hold building steady strength in the hips and thighs that power everyday lifting and floor-sitting. [evidence: isometric and tempo squat variants build lower-limb strength per ACSM; postpartum progression per ACOG and pelvic-health physiotherapy breath guidance.]
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: goddess

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Wide isometric squat/pulse. Same isometric-BP caveat as chair-pose. Knees track over toes in external rotation — fine if cued. The pulse variant reduces static-BP load. No impact.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Isometric and tempo squat variants build lower-limb strength per ACSM; postpartum progression per ACOG and pelvic-health physiotherapy breath guidance.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Add 'breathe steadily through the hold' (Valsalva/BP). Retain 'knees track over toes' (knee protection). Hypertension flag should temper long static holds in the scaffolded screening.

movements.name: Steady Reverse Lunge
movements.dose: No Sweat: 5-6 reps per side, step back and return slowly, 1 set, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 8-10 reps per side x 2 sets, rest 25s. Super Sweaty (screened): 10-12 reps per side x 3 sets with a 2s pause at the bottom, rest 20s.
movements.setup: Stand tall near a wall or chair for optional balance support, feet hip-width.
steps: Step one foot straight back, landing softly on the ball of that foot.
Lower the back knee toward the floor, front shin staying fairly vertical.
Keep most of your weight in your front heel and your chest tall.
Press through your front heel to step back to standing.
Move slowly and switch sides as cued; no jumping between legs.
veraCues: Step back soft, lower with control
Most of your weight in your front heel
Chest tall, back knee drops down
Press up slow, no need to rush
movements.breath: Inhale as you step back and lower, exhale as you press up to stand.
movements.regression: Static lunge holding the split stance, shallower range, or hold a chair for balance.
movements.progression: Deeper range, slower 3s lowering, or a 2s pause at the bottom; keep it controlled, never jumping (Super Sweaty only).

contraindications.why: A forward-drifting front knee or a hard back-knee landing stresses the joints.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the front shin vertical, lower the back knee gently, and reduce depth; do a static lunge instead.

contraindications.why: Letting the torso pitch forward loads the lumbar spine.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the chest tall over the hips and shorten the range.

contraindications.why: Single-leg loading with breath-holding raises asymmetric downward pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Exhale on the press up, keep range modest, and stop if you feel heaviness or leakage.

movements.intent: A knee-friendly single-leg strength builder, more forgiving than a forward lunge because the front knee stays stacked, training the carry-and-stair strength of daily life. [evidence: reverse lunges reduce anterior knee shear versus forward lunges (general biomechanics reasoning); load progression per ACSM, postpartum graded return per ACOG.]
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: low-lunge — identical knee-down split-stance geometry (kneel base) reads honestly for a reverse lunge; no new rig needed.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Single-leg strength; reverse direction keeps the front knee stacked, reducing anterior knee shear vs a forward lunge — a genuinely knee-friendlier choice. Risks are balance (fall) and front-knee tracking. No impact (no jumping). The bottom-pause super_sweaty variant is safe.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Reverse lunges reduce anterior knee shear vs forward lunges (general biomechanics reasoning); load progression per ACSM; postpartum graded return per ACOG.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Add 'light support available' for balance and 'front knee stays over the ankle.' Confirm no jumping/plyometric variant is ever added.

movements.name: Sit-to-Stand
movements.dose: No Sweat: 6-8 reps from a chair, tempo controlled, 1 set, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 10-12 reps x 2 sets, rest 25s. Super Sweaty (screened): 12-15 reps x 3 sets, lowering for 3s and hovering 1s before touching down, rest 20s.
movements.setup: Sit toward the front of a sturdy chair, feet flat and hip-width, slightly behind your knees.
steps: Lean your chest forward over your feet, nose toward toes.
Press through your heels and stand up tall without using your hands if you can.
Reach your hips back and lower slowly to lightly touch the seat.
Stay in control rather than dropping onto the chair.
Repeat at a steady, unhurried pace.
veraCues: Nose over toes, then press to stand
Push through your heels
Lower slow, kiss the seat
Hands free if you can, that is plenty
movements.breath: Exhale as you stand, inhale as you lower; exhale-on-effort keeps pressure off the floor.
movements.regression: Use your hands on your thighs or chair arms to assist, or use a higher seat.
movements.progression: Lower seat, slow the descent to 3s, or hover just above the seat before touching down (Super Sweaty only).

contraindications.why: A very low seat or fast descent increases knee load.
contraindications.substitution: Use a higher chair, slow the lowering, and assist with the hands.

contraindications.why: Standing by rounding and yanking the back loads the spine.
contraindications.substitution: Hinge from the hips with a tall chest and press through the heels.

contraindications.why: Breath-holding on the stand raises downward pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Exhale up, keep it smooth, and stop if you feel heaviness or doming.

contraindications.why: Many fast reps with breath-holding can spike blood pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Keep a steady pace and breathe through every rep.

movements.intent: The single most functional strength rehearsal for postpartum life: getting up off the floor or a chair with a baby in arms, scaled safely from a chair. [evidence: sit-to-stand is a validated functional lower-limb strength and rise-capacity exercise (clinical functional-assessment use); postpartum-appropriate and graded per ACOG.]
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: squats — stand base with hips sinking back and rising is the same visible motion as a chair stand; chair prop is described in caption, no new rig needed.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Chair-based functional lower-limb strength and rise-capacity drill — highly relevant to postpartum life and very safe (chair removes fall risk). The hover super_sweaty variant adds eccentric load benignly. Minor orthostatic note on repeated up-from-low transitions.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Sit-to-stand is a validated functional lower-limb strength and rise-capacity exercise (clinical functional-assessment use); postpartum-appropriate, graded per ACOG.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Cue 'exhale as you stand' (pelvic-floor pressure management). For the lower-seat super_sweaty variant, ensure the chair is stable. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Wall & Incline Push-Up
movements.dose: No Sweat: 6-8 wall reps, tempo 2s down 2s up, 1 set, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 10-12 reps x 2 sets, rest 25s. Super Sweaty (screened): incline reps (counter height) 10-12 x 3 sets, lowering for 3s, rest 20s.
movements.setup: Stand facing a wall (or a sturdy counter for more), hands slightly wider than shoulders at chest height.
steps: Step your feet back so your body leans toward the wall in a straight line.
Keep your body long from head to heels, ribs gently knitted down.
Inhale and bend your elbows to bring your chest toward the wall.
Exhale and press the wall away to straighten your arms.
Keep the movement smooth; the steeper the incline, the easier it is.
veraCues: Body long, one straight line
Bend the elbows, chest toward the wall
Exhale, press the wall away
Steeper means easier, your call
movements.breath: Inhale to lower toward the wall, exhale to press away; the exhale supports your core and floor.
movements.regression: Stand closer to upright at the wall, or take a wider hand stance and smaller range.
movements.progression: Lower the incline to a counter or sturdy table for more load (Super Sweaty only); never progress to a floor plank, which is off-limits postpartum.

contraindications.why: Weight-bearing through extended wrists can be uncomfortable.
contraindications.substitution: Push from the forearms against the wall, or grip the edge of a counter to neutralize the wrist.

contraindications.why: Flaring the elbows wide can pinch the shoulder.
contraindications.substitution: Keep elbows angled back toward the ribs at about 45 degrees and reduce range.

contraindications.why: This is the safe substitute for the forbidden plank; any sag or doming through the middle loads a healing midline.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the incline steep enough that the trunk stays long and braced gently with an exhale; stop if the belly domes.

movements.intent: A pressing-strength builder and the postpartum-safe stand-in for push-ups and planks, with load dialed entirely by the incline angle. [evidence: incline push-ups are a graded regression of the push-up pattern (ACSM resistance-training progression); chosen specifically to avoid the prohibited full plank per diastasis-recti safety consensus.]
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback — no standing-lean-to-wall rig exists and no wall prop is modeled; the avatar already degrades gracefully to captions, so a caption-only move is the honest, zero-pose-cost choice here.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Pressing-strength pattern with load set by incline angle — the explicit safe stand-in for floor push-ups/planks. Wrist extension under load is the chief joint flag (De Quervain's / wrist sensitivity) — incline reduces it vs floor. No impact. At counter height the anti-extension core demand rises but stays well below a floor plank.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Incline push-ups are a graded regression of the push-up pattern (ACSM resistance-training progression); chosen specifically to avoid the prohibited full plank per diastasis-recti safety consensus.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Hard-enforce 'never progress to a floor plank' (prog states this). Offer the higher wall version for wrist-sensitive users and pair with wrist-prep. Add 'keep the body in one line, low back not sagging' to protect the lumbar spine.

movements.name: Marching Bridge
movements.dose: No Sweat: hold a bridge and lift each foot 4-5cm for 2s, 4-6 alternating lifts, 1 set, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 8-10 alternating marches x 2 sets, rest 25s. Super Sweaty (screened): 10-12 marches per side x 2-3 sets with a fuller knee lift, rest 20s.
movements.setup: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat and hip-width, arms resting by your sides.
steps: Exhale and float your hips up into a smooth, level bridge.
Keep your hips even and steady, like balancing a tray across your pelvis.
Without dropping a hip, slowly lift one foot a small distance.
Set it down softly and lift the other; alternate with control.
Lower your hips down bone by bone to rest.
veraCues: Exhale, float the hips up level
Lift one foot, keep your hips still
Like balancing a tray on your pelvis
Slow and even, no rushing
movements.breath: Exhale as you lift each foot; keep breathing so you are not bracing hard against held breath.
movements.regression: Plain bridge hold with no marching, or smaller lifts keeping the toes light on the floor.
movements.progression: Lift the knee higher toward the chest while keeping the pelvis perfectly level (Super Sweaty only).

contraindications.why: Letting the back overarch at the top or a hip dropping during the march loads the lumbar spine.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the bridge low and level, ribs down, and reduce the foot lift; do a plain bridge.

contraindications.why: Single-leg load while bridged increases demand on the deep core and floor.
contraindications.substitution: Exhale on each lift, keep marches tiny, and stop if you feel doming or downward pressure.

contraindications.why: Feet placed too far away can strain the knees at the top.
contraindications.substitution: Bring the heels closer under the knees.

movements.intent: A progression of the trusted bridge that adds single-leg pelvic stability — exactly the control needed to carry a baby on one hip without the back complaining. [evidence: bridging and marching bridges are core-stability staples and are diastasis-recti-safe alternatives to crunches per pelvic-health physiotherapy consensus; load progression per ACSM.]
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: bridge — supine bridge hold is identical; the small foot/knee march reads acceptably on the existing bridge keyframes, so no new rig is needed.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Bridge with single-leg march — adds single-leg pelvic stability. Zero spinal flexion, diastasis-safe. Key flag: the pelvis must not drop/rotate or the low back arch as one foot lifts; otherwise it loads the lumbar spine and the obliques can dome. Supine position fine for postpartum.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Bridging and marching bridges are core-stability staples and diastasis-recti-safe alternatives to crunches per pelvic health physiotherapy consensus; load per ACSM.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Make 'keep the pelvis perfectly level — if it tips or your belly domes, take smaller lifts' an explicit spoken cue. Otherwise approved; correctly gated at super_sweaty for the fuller knee lift.

movements.name: Standing Row
movements.dose: No Sweat: 8-10 slow isometric rows, squeeze and hold 2s, 1 set, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 12-15 reps x 2 sets, rest 25s. Super Sweaty (screened): banded rows 12-15 x 3 sets, lowering for 3s, rest 20s.
movements.setup: Stand tall, soft knees, hinge forward slightly from the hips with a flat back; hold a band or simply make fists.
steps: Reach your arms forward at chest height, knuckles facing down.
Draw your elbows back along your ribs as if pulling a drawer open.
Squeeze your shoulder blades gently together at the back of the row.
Hold the squeeze, then release your arms forward with control.
If using a band, anchor it ahead of you and keep tension throughout.
veraCues: Pull your elbows back along your ribs
Squeeze the shoulder blades together
Open the chest, drop the shoulders
Release forward slow and controlled
movements.breath: Exhale as you draw the elbows back, inhale as you release forward.
movements.regression: Pure isometric: press your fists back against an imaginary load with no band, or sit tall to reduce the hinge.
movements.progression: Use a heavier band or slow the release to 3s for more back-strength challenge (Super Sweaty only).

contraindications.why: Shrugging up or yanking the band can irritate the shoulder.
contraindications.substitution: Keep shoulders down away from the ears and use a lighter band or pure isometric squeeze.

contraindications.why: A rounded-back hinge loads the lumbar spine.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce the forward hinge or perform tall and seated with the band; keep the back flat.

contraindications.why: Gripping hard and holding the breath during isometric pulls can raise blood pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Lighten the grip, shorten the squeeze, and keep breathing.

movements.intent: Strengthen the pulling muscles of the upper back to counter the rounded-forward posture of feeding and carrying, with no equipment required. [evidence: rowing strengthens scapular retractors to balance habitual flexed posture (general musculoskeletal reasoning); resistance progression via band per ACSM; postpartum-appropriate.]
movements.posesNote: new keyframe rig — pulling motion (elbow flexion drawing back, scapular retraction) is a distinct, frequently-reused pattern not captured by any existing rig; one stand-base rig earns its cost across all upper-body row content.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Scapular-retraction pulling pattern, isometric or banded. Counters postpartum forward-rounded posture — valuable. Minimal joint risk; banded version load is self-selected. Isometric squeeze-holds carry a minor BP/Valsalva note. Hip-hinge posture during rows must keep a neutral spine.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Rowing strengthens scapular retractors to balance habitual flexed posture (general musculoskeletal reasoning); resistance progression via band per ACSM; postpartum-appropriate.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Cue 'soft knees, flat back, breathe through the squeeze.' If performed in a hinged stance, ensure the hinge is neutral-spine (see hip-hinge cues). Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Hip Hinge
movements.dose: No Sweat: 6-8 slow hinges, tempo 3s down 2s up, 1 set, rest 30s. Slightly Sweaty: 10-12 reps x 2 sets, rest 25s. Super Sweaty (screened): 12-15 reps x 3 sets with a 2s pause at the bottom, rest 20s.
movements.setup: Stand with feet hip-width, knees softly bent, hands resting on the fronts of your thighs.
steps: Keeping your back flat and long, push your hips straight back toward the wall behind you.
Let your hands slide down your thighs as your chest lowers, knees only softly bent.
Hinge until you feel a stretch in the backs of your legs, back staying flat.
Exhale and squeeze your glutes to drive your hips forward back to standing.
Stand tall without overarching at the top.
veraCues: Push your hips back, back stays flat
Slide your hands down your thighs
Feel it in the backs of your legs
Squeeze your glutes to stand tall
movements.breath: Inhale as you hinge back, exhale as you drive your hips forward to stand; this is the lifting breath for daily life.
movements.regression: Smaller range with hands on thighs, or hinge back to lightly tap a chair behind you.
movements.progression: Greater range, slower 4s lowering, or hold a light household object close to the body (Super Sweaty only).

contraindications.why: Rounding the back instead of hinging at the hips loads the lumbar discs — this is the most common lifting-injury pattern.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce range, keep the back flat, and hinge to a chair tap; stop if the back rounds.

contraindications.why: Turning the hinge into a squat by over-bending the knees changes the target and can load the knees.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the knees only softly bent and push the hips back, not down.

contraindications.why: Breath-holding while bending forward raises downward pressure on the floor.
contraindications.substitution: Exhale on the way up, keep range modest, and stop if you feel heaviness or doming.

contraindications.why: Straining with held breath while bent over can raise blood pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Keep breathing and avoid maximal effort.

movements.intent: Teach the flat-backed hip hinge — the single most important pattern for lifting a car seat or a toddler without hurting the back. [evidence: the hip hinge is the foundational safe-lifting pattern in injury-prevention and occupational-health guidance (general consensus); postpartum graded loading per ACOG and pelvic-health physiotherapy breath cues.]
movements.posesNote: new keyframe rig — a flat-back hinge (spine bend at the hip with a neutral back, distinct from the rounding fold and the squat) is not represented by any existing rig and is a foundational pattern worth one stand-base rig.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: The flat-backed hip hinge is the single most protective real-life pattern for postpartum lifting (car seats, toddlers) — high value. The whole point is to AVOID lumbar flexion under load, so cueing a truly neutral spine is essential; a rounded hinge would load the lumbar discs. Light household-object load (super_sweaty) is fine if held close to the body. No impact.
vetting.evidenceBasis: The hip hinge is the foundational safe-lifting pattern in injury-prevention/occupational-health guidance (general consensus); postpartum graded loading per ACOG and pelvic-health physiotherapy breath cues.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Make 'hinge from the hips with a long, flat back — bottom travels back, not down' the primary cue, and 'exhale as you stand' for pelvic-floor pressure. Keep any load light and close to the body. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Standing March
movements.dose: Slightly Sweaty: 60s, ~110-120 steps/min, 1 set, 20s rest into next move. No Sweat: 45s at an easy stroll pace, no rest needed.
movements.setup: Stand tall, feet hip-width, weight even through both feet. Soft knees, ribs stacked over hips, crown lifting.
steps: Lift one knee to a comfortable height, foot stays low.
Place it down with control, then lift the other knee.
Let the opposite arm swing naturally with each knee.
Find a steady rhythm you could hold while talking.
veraCues: Lift one knee, set it down softly
Let your arms swing easy and natural
Keep a pace where you could still chat
Stand tall, crown of your head lifting
movements.breath: Breathe steadily through the rhythm; gently draw the lower belly in on each knee lift, never holding your breath.
movements.regression: Toe taps: keep one foot lightly grounded and tap the other toes forward instead of lifting the knee, lowering intensity and balance demand.
movements.progression: High-knee march (slightly_sweaty/super_sweaty): lift knees higher toward hip height and quicken the cadence, keeping both feet low and contact soft — no hop.

contraindications.why: Single-leg loading and faster cadence can challenge balance, especially early postpartum or with fatigue.
contraindications.substitution: Hold a chair back or counter with one hand, or use the toe-tap regression.

contraindications.why: Even low-impact marching loads the pelvic floor cyclically; symptoms signal the load is currently too high.
contraindications.substitution: Drop to toe taps at a slow cadence and reduce knee height; pause exhale-on-lift; if symptoms persist, refer to a pelvic-health physiotherapist.

contraindications.why: Doming/coning along the midline indicates the deep core is not yet managing this load.
contraindications.substitution: Lower the knees and slow down so the belly stays flat; favour the toe-tap regression.

movements.intent: A friendly, accessible way to lift the heart rate and warm the whole body without any impact — the gateway cardio move that everyone can do.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig tree-pose — its single-leg-lift standing keyframes read naturally as a knee-lift march; honest visual match and avoids a costly new rig.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Standing march, both feet low, no impact — the universal gateway cardio. The high-knee progression must keep contact soft and 'no hop' (stated). No BP or joint concern.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Low-impact aerobic activity consistent with ACOG postpartum activity guidance and the app's no-jump rule.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Retain 'both feet low, no hop' at the high-knee tempo. Otherwise approved. Correctly not super_sweaty by itself (high-knee progression carries the higher tier).

movements.name: Step-Touch
movements.dose: Slightly Sweaty: 60s continuous, ~1 step-touch per second, 1 set, 15-20s rest. No Sweat: 45s slow and small.
movements.setup: Stand with feet together, knees soft, hands resting at your sides or on your hips.
steps: Step your right foot out to the side.
Bring your left foot in to lightly tap beside it.
Step back out to the left and tap the right in.
Keep a gentle side-to-side sway, both feet quiet on the floor.
veraCues: Step wide, then tap your feet together
Stay low and quiet, no bouncing
Let a gentle sway carry you side to side
Add a soft arm swing if it feels good
movements.breath: Easy, continuous breathing in time with the sway; no breath-holding.
movements.regression: Make the steps very small and slow, or perform seated in a chair tapping feet side to side, to keep heart rate gentle and remove balance load.
movements.progression: Add an overhead arm reach with each step out, or widen and quicken the steps (slightly_sweaty), increasing range and heart-rate demand without impact.

contraindications.why: Repeated lateral weight shifts load the knees side-to-side.
contraindications.substitution: Narrow the step width and slow the tempo; keep knees tracking over toes.

contraindications.why: Lateral loading still transmits force through the pelvic floor at faster tempos.
contraindications.substitution: Slow the pace, keep steps small, exhale gently as you step out; seated version if symptoms continue.

movements.intent: A rhythmic, low-skill side-to-side move that warms the body and gently raises heart rate — easy to follow even while distracted.
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback — lateral weight-shift footwork has no existing rig and the side-to-side travel is hard to read on the fixed avatar; captions plus the existing graceful fallback are honest here, deferring rig cost.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Rhythmic side-to-side, one foot always grounded, no impact. Low skill, low risk. Overhead-arm progression adds mild BP/shoulder demand only.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Low-impact aerobic warm-up/conditioning consistent with ACOG and the no-jump rule.
vetting.modificationsRequired: None.

movements.name: Step-Backs
movements.dose: Slightly Sweaty: 50-60s alternating, ~1 step-back per second, 1 set, 20s rest.
movements.setup: Stand tall, feet hip-width, core gently engaged, arms ready to swing.
steps: Step one foot back into a small, shallow lunge.
Press through the front heel to return to standing.
Step the other foot back the same way.
Keep the front knee soft and the torso tall throughout.
veraCues: Step back, tap lightly, and return
Press through your front heel to stand tall
Keep your chest lifted, no leaning forward
Find a steady, repeatable rhythm
movements.breath: Exhale as you press back up to standing (exhale on the effort); inhale as you step back.
movements.regression: Reduce the step-back depth to a near-stationary tap, or hold a chair for support, lowering both intensity and balance demand.
movements.progression: Add a knee drive: as you return to standing, drive the back knee up toward hip height (links to standing-knee-drives), raising heart rate (slightly_sweaty/super_sweaty).

contraindications.why: Loaded shallow lunging stresses the front knee, especially if it drifts past the toes.
contraindications.substitution: Keep steps shallow and weight in the front heel; reduce to a stationary tap-back.

contraindications.why: Stepping backward removes visual reference and challenges stability.
contraindications.substitution: Hold a chair or counter; keep one hand light for reassurance.

contraindications.why: Repeated step-and-return cycles load the pelvic floor.
contraindications.substitution: Slow the tempo, shallow the range, exhale on the press up; refer to pelvic-health PT if symptoms persist.

movements.intent: A gentle alternating step that builds leg strength and elevates heart rate while keeping both feet in contact with the ground — no impact.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig low-lunge — its lunge keyframes read as the step-back position; with mirrorHalfway it alternates sides honestly, avoiding a new rig.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Alternating step-back, both feet contact, no impact. The knee-drive progression raises HR without a hop. Minor balance demand only.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Low-impact aerobic conditioning consistent with ACOG and the no-jump rule.
vetting.modificationsRequired: None. Confirm 'no hop' carries into the knee-drive progression.

movements.name: Light Fast Feet
movements.dose: Super Sweaty: 20s fast / 20s recovery march, repeat 2-3 rounds within the main block. 30s total rest before next move.
movements.setup: Stand with feet just inside hip-width, knees soft, weight on the balls of the feet, slight forward lean from the ankles.
steps: Make small, quick steps in place, feet barely leaving the floor.
Keep contact light and low — shuffle quickly, never hop.
Pump the arms gently to match the foot speed.
Slow to a march for the recovery, then repeat.
veraCues: Quick little feet, stay low to the floor
Light and fast — no hopping, no bouncing
Pump your arms to keep the rhythm
Slow to a march to catch your breath
movements.breath: Breathe rhythmically and audibly; never hold the breath through a fast burst. If you cannot speak a short sentence on recovery, slow down.
movements.regression: Reduce to a brisk standing march at a steady pace, removing the fast burst entirely while keeping heart rate up.
movements.progression: Lengthen the fast interval to 30s and shorten recovery to 15s, increasing the cardio stimulus while keeping feet low and impact-free.

contraindications.why: Fast cyclical foot speed is the highest pelvic-floor load in this bank; it must only unlock after the Super Sweaty screening gate (PAR-Q+/CSEP-style readiness plus pelvic-floor symptom check) is passed.
contraindications.substitution: If the gate is not passed, serve the brisk-march regression at Slightly Sweaty intensity instead.

contraindications.why: Rapid repeated foot contact maximises intra-abdominal pressure cycling. [evidence: thin — flag] Pelvic-health PT consensus supports symptom-led load progression rather than fixed timelines.
contraindications.substitution: Stop the fast interval, return to a slow march, and signpost pelvic-health physiotherapy.

contraindications.why: Fast pace makes it hard to maintain deep-core control; visible coning means the load exceeds current capacity.
contraindications.substitution: Drop to a controlled march where the belly stays flat.

movements.intent: An interval-style burst that genuinely elevates heart rate for the Super Sweaty tier while keeping both feet low and contact soft — vigorous without impact.
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback — rapid small-amplitude foot speed cannot be conveyed by the keyframe avatar at honest fidelity; captions plus tempo cueing carry it, deferring an expensive bespoke rig.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Interval-style fast feet (20s fast / 20s recovery) — this is the most genuinely VIGOROUS cardio in the bank and is correctly super_sweaty-only. Even kept low and soft-contact, rapid repetitive ground contact raises intra-abdominal pressure and pelvic-floor load; women with stress incontinence or early postpartum may leak or feel heaviness. Provided contact stays soft and there is no hop, impact is minimal but not zero.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Interval low-impact conditioning is within ACSM vigorous-intensity definitions; pelvic-floor impact-loading caution per pelvic health physiotherapy consensus (return-to-impact is staged) [evidence: precise readiness criteria thin — flag].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Keep the super_sweaty screening gate HARD. Add an explicit pelvic-floor self-check cue ('if you feel any leaking or heaviness, slow to a march') and offer step-touch/marching as the instant regression. Confirm 'feet stay low, soft contact, no hop' is enforced. This is the move most worth flagging for the scaffolded incontinence/early-postpartum screen.

movements.name: Standing Knee Drives
movements.dose: Slightly Sweaty: 40s per side, controlled, 1 set. Super Sweaty: 45s per side at a quicker, rhythmic tempo. 20s rest between sides.
movements.setup: Stand tall, step one foot slightly back, reach both arms up and forward, ribs stacked over pelvis.
steps: Drive your back knee up toward hip height.
At the same time, draw your arms down toward the lifted knee.
Lower the foot back with control, arms reaching up again.
Repeat on one side, then switch.
veraCues: Drive your knee up, pull your arms down
Exhale as the knee comes up
Stand tall, keep the standing leg strong
Lower with control, no flopping down
movements.breath: Exhale on the knee drive (the exertion), drawing the lower belly gently in; inhale as you reach the arms back up. This exhale-on-effort timing protects the pelvic floor.
movements.regression: Lower the knee height and remove the arm pull, or hold a chair with one hand — keeps the pattern with far less load and better balance.
movements.progression: Quicken the tempo into a rhythmic drive (super_sweaty), increasing heart rate while keeping the standing foot planted and no hop.

contraindications.flag: Super Sweaty screening gate (required for super_sweaty tempo)
contraindications.why: Fast repeated knee drives raise intra-abdominal pressure; the quick variant depends on passing the Super Sweaty screening gate.
contraindications.substitution: Serve the slow, controlled Slightly Sweaty version if the gate is not passed.

contraindications.why: Each knee drive loads the pelvic floor, more so at speed.
contraindications.substitution: Slow down, lower knee height, ensure exhale-on-drive; refer to pelvic-health PT if symptoms persist.

contraindications.why: The reach-up-then-pull-down can encourage lumbar extension if the ribs flare.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the arm reach lower and ribs knitted down; engage the core before each drive.

contraindications.why: Single-leg loading with arm movement challenges stability.
contraindications.substitution: Hold a chair; reduce knee height.

movements.intent: A full-body knee-and-arm drive that raises heart rate and builds coordinated core control, scalable from controlled to vigorous without any impact.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig tree-pose — the single-leg knee-lift keyframes carry the drive; sided so it serves per-side honestly, sparing a new rig.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Standing knee-and-arm drive, standing foot planted, no hop. Raises HR and trains coordination. Balance demand is moderate at the quicker tempo. No impact if the planted-foot cue holds.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Low-impact conditioning consistent with ACOG and the no-jump rule.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Retain 'standing foot stays planted, no hop.' Offer light support for balance at the vigorous tempo. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Lateral Steps
movements.dose: Slightly Sweaty: 50-60s travelling side to side, 1 set, 20s rest. Super Sweaty: add a low half-squat each direction for 45-60s.
movements.setup: Stand with feet hip-width, knees soft, hips slightly back into a quarter-squat, chest lifted.
steps: Take two or three steps to one side, staying low.
Keep your weight back in your heels and knees soft.
Travel back the other way with the same low, controlled steps.
Keep the chest lifted and breathe steadily.
veraCues: Stay low and step side to side
Weight in your heels, chest lifted
Smooth and controlled, no bouncing
Push the floor away with each step
movements.breath: Steady continuous breathing; if adding the squat, exhale as you press up out of each low step.
movements.regression: Stand more upright and take smaller, slower steps without the squat, reducing leg and heart-rate demand.
movements.progression: Deepen into a half-squat with each direction change and quicken the travel (super_sweaty), raising heart rate and glute load without impact.

contraindications.flag: Super Sweaty screening gate (required for squat-loaded variant)
contraindications.why: The faster, deeper variant raises intra-abdominal pressure and depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
contraindications.substitution: Serve the upright Slightly Sweaty step if the gate is not passed.

contraindications.why: Quarter-squatting while shifting laterally loads the knees.
contraindications.substitution: Stay shallower and slower; keep knees tracking over the toes.

contraindications.why: Lateral loading plus squat depth increases pelvic-floor demand.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce depth and speed; exhale on the press up; refer to pelvic-health PT if symptoms persist.

movements.intent: A low, athletic side-to-side travel that warms the hips and lifts heart rate while keeping both feet grounded — impact-free conditioning.
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback — lateral travel reads poorly on the static avatar and there is no honest existing rig for travelling steps; captions carry it via the existing graceful fallback.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Grounded lateral travel with a half-squat option, no impact. Glute/HR load scales safely. The half-squat super_sweaty adds knee/glute load that is self-limited.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Low-impact conditioning consistent with ACOG and the no-jump rule.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Cue 'knees track over toes' on the half-squat. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Shadow Boxing
movements.dose: Slightly Sweaty: 60s of alternating punches at a steady pace, 1 set, 20s rest. Super Sweaty: 45s faster combinations.
movements.setup: Stand with feet staggered, one slightly forward, knees soft, hands up near your chin, elbows in, core gently braced.
steps: Punch one hand forward at chest height, rotating slightly through the torso.
Bring it back to guard as the other hand punches.
Keep your knees soft and feet quietly planted.
Find a steady boxing rhythm, breathing out on each punch.
veraCues: Punch forward, then back to guard
Exhale sharply with each punch
Keep your knees soft, feet planted
Let your waist rotate, stay tall
movements.breath: Short exhale on each punch (exhale on exertion); keep breathing rhythmically and never brace silently against a held breath.
movements.regression: Slow the punches and make them smaller, or perform seated in a chair, keeping the upper-body cardio without standing or rotational load.
movements.progression: Add hooks and uppercuts in faster combinations and add a gentle knee pivot (super_sweaty), raising heart rate while feet stay grounded.

contraindications.flag: Super Sweaty screening gate (required for fast combinations)
contraindications.why: Faster, forceful punching with trunk rotation raises intra-abdominal pressure; the vigorous variant depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
contraindications.substitution: Serve slow, controlled single punches at Slightly Sweaty if the gate is not passed.

contraindications.why: Repeated punching loads the shoulder, especially at speed or with prior shoulder issues.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce range and speed; keep punches at chest height, not overhead; stop if pinching.

contraindications.why: Trunk rotation and forceful exhale can spike abdominal pressure and challenge midline control.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce rotation and force, keep the exhale soft, brace the deep core lightly before each punch; refer to pelvic-health PT if doming or symptoms occur.

contraindications.why: Snapping the punch can jar the wrist joint.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the wrist neutral and avoid full lockout; slow the punch.

movements.intent: An empowering upper-body cardio move that lifts heart rate and channels energy, with both feet planted and zero impact — a confidence builder.
movements.posesNote: new keyframe rig — punching arms are central to the move and no existing rig animates forward arm strikes; a focused upper-body rig is justified because the limb pattern is unique and core to comprehension.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Standing punches, feet planted, zero impact — empowering and safe for the lower body/pelvic floor. The chief joint flag is the SHOULDER and ELBOW: rapid end-range punching with full elbow lockout can strain the elbow and shoulder, especially with lax connective tissue. Trunk rotation at speed is generally well tolerated unloaded.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Upper-body cardio is low-impact and pelvic-floor-friendly; elbow-lockout caution is general musculoskeletal reasoning [evidence: thin on a named source — flag].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Add 'do not snap the elbows fully straight — keep a soft bend' and 'punch with control, not maximum speed' at the faster super_sweaty tempo. Otherwise approved with feet planted.

movements.name: Wall Mountain-Climbers
movements.dose: Slightly Sweaty: 40s controlled, alternating knees, 1 set, 25s rest. Super Sweaty: 30s quicker, 2 rounds with 30s rest.
movements.setup: Stand an arm's length from a wall (or sturdy counter). Place both hands flat on the wall at shoulder height, step your feet back into a gentle incline lean, body in one long line from head to heels.
steps: Brace your core gently so your back stays long, not sagging.
Draw one knee up toward your chest.
Return it and draw the other knee up.
Keep alternating at a steady, controlled pace.
veraCues: Long line from head to heels
Draw one knee up, then the other
Exhale as the knee comes in
Keep your hips quiet, no bouncing
movements.breath: Exhale as the knee draws in (exhale on exertion); inhale as the foot returns. Maintain gentle deep-core engagement throughout — this is the safe, upright alternative to a floor plank.
movements.regression: Stand more upright (hands higher on the wall, feet closer in) so the incline is shallower and the core load is light; slow the knee tempo.
movements.progression: Walk the feet further back to increase the incline and quicken the knee drive (super_sweaty), raising heart rate while never reaching a full horizontal plank.

contraindications.flag: Super Sweaty screening gate (required for faster/steeper variant)
contraindications.why: A steeper incline and faster cadence raise core and pelvic-floor load; the vigorous variant depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
contraindications.substitution: Keep upright and slow at Slightly Sweaty if the gate is not passed.

contraindications.why: This is deliberately an incline, NOT a full plank, to keep abdominal pressure manageable per the postpartum no-full-plank rule; even so, doming or leaking signals too much load. [evidence: ACOG postpartum guidance supports graded core loading led by symptoms.]
contraindications.substitution: Raise the hands higher (more upright) until the belly stays flat and no symptoms occur; favour bird-dog or breath-led core instead.

contraindications.why: Weight-bearing through flat hands loads the wrists.
contraindications.substitution: Place forearms on the wall instead of hands, or use a higher surface to reduce the lean.

contraindications.why: The incline lean loads the shoulders.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce the lean by standing more upright; stop if pinching.

movements.intent: A postpartum-safe, plank-free way to get a climber-style cardio and core stimulus using a wall — vigorous-capable while honouring the no-full-plank rule.
movements.posesNote: new keyframe rig — the wall-incline lean with alternating knee draw is a distinct body position no existing rig represents; a rig is justified because misleading reuse could imply a floor plank, which violates the safety envelope.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Wall (incline) mountain-climbers — a plank-free climber stimulus, honouring the no-full-plank rule. The steeper the incline, the more it approaches plank-family anti-extension load and the higher the doming/pelvic-floor pressure and wrist load. The alternating-knee drive at speed raises intra-abdominal pressure. Correctly gated to slightly/super tiers.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Plank-free wall climber is consistent with diastasis safety consensus (avoids the prohibited full plank); conditioning per ACSM [evidence: thin on a named protocol — flag].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Hard-enforce 'never walk the feet back to a full horizontal plank' (prog states this — enforce at assembler). Add doming self-check and 'keep breathing' cues. Pair with wrist-prep. Higher (more vertical) wall position should be the slightly_sweaty default; lower incline reserved for screened super_sweaty.

movements.name: Step-Out Jacks
movements.dose: Slightly Sweaty: 50-60s at a steady step rhythm, 1 set, 20s rest. Super Sweaty: 45s quicker tempo, 2 rounds.
movements.setup: Stand with feet together, knees soft, arms down by your sides.
steps: Step one foot out wide as you sweep both arms up overhead.
Step it back in as the arms lower.
Step the other foot out wide, arms sweeping up again.
Keep one foot grounded at all times — step, never jump.
veraCues: Step wide, sweep your arms up
Step in, arms come down
One foot always stays on the floor
Big arms, soft knees, no hopping
movements.breath: Steady rhythmic breathing; gently exhale as the arms sweep up. No breath-holding through the rhythm.
movements.regression: Keep the arms low (chest height instead of overhead) and slow the steps, or just step the feet without the arm sweep — reduces shoulder and heart-rate demand.
movements.progression: Quicken the step tempo and add a small heel-raise as the foot steps out (super_sweaty), raising heart rate while keeping a foot grounded — still no jump.

contraindications.flag: Super Sweaty screening gate (required for fast variant)
contraindications.why: Faster stepping with overhead arms raises intra-abdominal pressure; the vigorous tempo depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
contraindications.substitution: Serve the steady, arms-low Slightly Sweaty version if the gate is not passed.

contraindications.why: Repeated overhead arm sweeps load the shoulders.
contraindications.substitution: Keep arms at chest height or out to a T rather than fully overhead.

contraindications.why: The step-out rhythm and overhead reach increase pelvic-floor load.
contraindications.substitution: Slow down, keep arms low, exhale on the reach; refer to pelvic-health PT if symptoms persist.

contraindications.why: Repeated overhead arm movement can transiently raise blood pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Keep arms at or below shoulder height and maintain steady breathing; avoid breath-holding.

movements.intent: A no-jump take on the classic jumping jack that delivers the same full-body cardio rhythm while keeping one foot grounded — impact-free and pelvic-floor-considerate.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig goddess — its wide-stance, arms-engaged standing keyframes read as the step-out-with-arms shape; honest match that avoids a new rig.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: No-jump jacks with one foot always grounded — the explicit pelvic-floor-considerate alternative to jumping jacks. Honours the no-jump rule. The heel-raise progression keeps contact soft. Shoulder overhead-arm reach is the only minor flag (BP/shoulder).
vetting.evidenceBasis: Low-impact alternative to jumping jacks consistent with ACOG and the app's no-jump rule; pelvic-floor-considerate by design.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Confirm 'one foot always grounded, no jump' carries into the quicker super_sweaty tempo. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Skater Steps
movements.dose: Slightly Sweaty: 50s alternating at a controlled pace, 1 set, 20s rest. Super Sweaty: 45s quicker with a deeper reach, 2 rounds with 30s rest.
movements.setup: Stand with feet hip-width, soft knees, slight hinge forward from the hips, chest lifted.
steps: Step one foot back and across behind you into a curtsy-style stance, no hop.
Sweep the opposite arm across as you step.
Press back to centre, then step the other foot back and across.
Stay low and controlled, both feet always in contact with the floor.
veraCues: Step back and across, like a slow skate
Stay low, sweep your arm with you
Press back to centre with control
Feet stay grounded — no hopping
movements.breath: Exhale as you press back to centre (the effort); inhale into the step-across. Keep the breath flowing.
movements.regression: Reduce the cross-behind to a small step-back without crossing, and stand taller — lowers balance and knee demand.
movements.progression: Deepen the curtsy and quicken the alternation with a fuller arm reach (super_sweaty), raising heart rate while keeping feet grounded — the no-hop alternative to skater hops.

contraindications.flag: Super Sweaty screening gate (required for deeper/faster variant)
contraindications.why: The deeper, quicker crossover raises intra-abdominal pressure and balance demand; the vigorous variant depends on the Super Sweaty screening gate.
contraindications.substitution: Serve the shallow, non-crossing Slightly Sweaty version if the gate is not passed.

contraindications.why: Crossing behind under load can rotate and stress the knee.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce depth, avoid crossing fully behind, keep the knee tracking forward over the foot.

contraindications.why: The crossover stance narrows the base of support.
contraindications.substitution: Hold a chair with one hand; keep the step shallow and uncrossed.

contraindications.why: Repeated lateral loading transmits force through the pelvic floor.
contraindications.substitution: Slow the pace and shallow the range; exhale on the press to centre; refer to pelvic-health PT if symptoms persist.

movements.intent: A graceful, grounded skater pattern that lifts heart rate and challenges lateral strength and balance without the jump or impact of a true skater hop.
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback — the lateral crossover with travel is not represented by any existing rig and reads poorly on the static avatar; captions plus graceful fallback are the honest, low-cost choice.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Grounded skater/curtsy pattern, no hop — the no-impact alternative to plyometric skater hops. Lateral strength/balance challenge. The curtsy step crosses the midline, which can stress the knee if uncontrolled; deeper/faster super_sweaty raises that.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Low-impact lateral conditioning consistent with ACOG and the no-jump rule.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Cue 'keep the front knee tracking forward, do not let it collapse inward' on the curtsy, and confirm 'feet stay grounded, no hop.' Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Heel Digs
movements.dose: No Sweat: 45s easy alternating digs. Slightly Sweaty: 60s with arm pull-downs at a brisker pace, 1 set, 15-20s rest.
movements.setup: Stand with feet hip-width, knees soft, weight in one foot, arms loosely up in front.
steps: Reach one heel forward and dig it lightly into the floor.
As you do, draw both arms down and back, like pulling on a rope.
Bring the foot back to centre, arms reaching up again.
Alternate heels in a steady, low rhythm.
veraCues: Dig one heel forward, pull your arms back
Keep it low and rhythmic, no bouncing
Switch heels at a comfortable pace
Soft knees, tall chest, easy breath
movements.breath: Easy continuous breathing; exhale gently on the arm pull-down. No breath-holding.
movements.regression: Remove the arms and slow the heel digs, or perform seated tapping heels forward — keeps the gentle warm-up stimulus with minimal load and no balance demand.
movements.progression: Quicken the tempo and add a bigger arm pull (slightly_sweaty), raising heart rate while both feet stay low and grounded.

contraindications.why: Shifting weight to one foot to reach the other heel forward challenges balance.
contraindications.substitution: Hold a chair with one hand, or use the seated version.

contraindications.why: Even gentle weight transfer cycles load through the pelvic floor at a brisk pace.
contraindications.substitution: Slow the tempo and keep digs small; exhale on the arm pull; seated version if symptoms continue.

contraindications.why: The arm pull-down can encourage leaning back if the core is not engaged.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the ribs stacked over the pelvis and the core gently braced; reduce the arm range.

movements.intent: A gentle, low-skill warm-up and cardio move pairing alternating heel reaches with an arm pull — accessible, grounded, and easy on the pelvic floor.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig forward-fold — its standing-with-forward-reach keyframes approximate the heel-dig-and-pull shape adequately; honest enough for a low-intensity move, avoiding a new rig. (If visual fidelity proves weak in review, downgrade to caption-only — no safety risk either way.)

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Alternating heel reaches with an arm pull, both feet low, no impact — gentle, low-skill, pelvic-floor-easy. Negligible risk.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Low-impact warm-up/cardio consistent with ACOG and the no-jump rule.
vetting.modificationsRequired: None.

movements.name: Downward Dog
movements.dose: Hold 45 s default (no_sweat: hold 60 s soft and still; slightly_sweaty: 45 s with slow heel pedals; super_sweaty: 3-5 breaths then step to low-lunge and repeat, 2-3 rounds, requires Super Sweaty screening gate). Tempo slow, breath-led. Rest 6 s transition (TRANSITION_SECS) into next move.
movements.setup: Start on hands and knees, hands under shoulders, knees under hips, fingers spread wide.
steps: Tuck your toes and press your hips up and back toward an upside-down V.
Keep your knees bent generously so your back can lengthen.
Press the floor away through your whole hand to take weight out of your wrists.
Let your head hang heavy between your arms.
Slowly pedal one heel and then the other if it feels good.
veraCues: Press the floor away, soft knees
Bend your knees as much as you like
Pedal your heels slowly
Let your head hang heavy
movements.breath: Breathe smooth and even through the hold; exhale as you press the hips back, inhale to lengthen the spine. No breath-holding.
movements.regression: Puppy pose at the wall or on hands-and-knees: walk hands forward, melt chest toward the floor, hips over knees. Same shoulder and upper-back opening with zero wrist load and no inversion (helpful with hypertension).
movements.progression: Down-dog to low-lunge step-throughs (within slightly/super_sweaty): from the hold, step one foot forward to a lunge, return, alternate. Raises heart rate while staying low-impact. Super-Sweaty pacing requires the Super Sweaty screening gate.

contraindications.flag: wrists (carpal tunnel, postpartum wrist pain / 'mommy thumb' de Quervain's)
contraindications.why: Full weight-bearing through extended wrists is a common pain trigger and de Quervain's tenosynovitis is notably more frequent in the postpartum/lifting period.
contraindications.substitution: Forearm down-dog (dolphin) on forearms, or the puppy-pose regression, removing the loaded wrist extension.

contraindications.flag: hypertension / late-pregnancy or very early postpartum
contraindications.why: Head-below-heart inversions can be uncomfortable or unwise with high blood pressure. [evidence: thin — flag] General caution; clinical reasoning: avoid sustained inversion if BP is uncontrolled.
contraindications.substitution: Tabletop or puppy pose keeping the head level with or above the heart.

contraindications.why: Sustained overhead-loaded shoulder flexion under body weight can aggravate an irritable shoulder.
contraindications.substitution: Shorten the hold, bend the elbows slightly, or use the tabletop regression.

movements.intent: A whole-body decompression stretch that gives the back and shoulders breathing room after carrying and bending, while gently waking the calves and hamstrings. Preserves the existing 'big upside-down stretch' spirit.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: down-dog

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Inverted whole-body decompression. Three flags: (1) WRIST weight-bearing in extension under significant load — the most wrist-demanding hold in the bank (De Quervain's relevance); (2) head-below-heart inversion raises a BP/light-headedness consideration, relevant for a hypertension flag or dizziness; (3) the super_sweaty step-to-low-lunge flow raises HR safely but increases wrist transitions. Not a crunch; diastasis-neutral.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Whole-body stretch; wrist-loading and mild-inversion BP cautions are general musculoskeletal/physiological reasoning [evidence: thin on a named guideline — flag]. Super-sweaty flow gated by the screening gate.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Offer a forearm/lower-wrist-friendly or knees-on-floor regression and pair with wrist-prep. Add 'come up slowly' and a note for anyone prone to dizziness/high BP. Keep heels-bent option (already cued). Confirm the super_sweaty flow keeps soft, grounded steps (no hop).

movements.name: Low Lunge
movements.dose: Hold 50 s per side default (no_sweat: 60 s settled, back knee padded; slightly_sweaty: 50 s with a slow forward-and-back rock; super_sweaty: used as a transition pose inside a flow rather than a long hold). Tempo slow. Rest 6 s transition between sides.
movements.setup: From hands and knees, step one foot forward between your hands; lower the back knee to the mat and pad it if needed.
steps: Step the front foot forward so the knee stacks over the ankle.
Lower the back knee down and let the top of the back foot rest on the mat.
Let your hips melt gently forward until you feel a soft stretch at the front of the back hip.
Lift your chest and let your shoulders soften down.
If it feels good, sweep your arms up alongside your ears.
veraCues: Back knee down, settle in
Let your hips melt gently forward
Lift your chest, soft shoulders
Pad that knee if it complains
movements.breath: Slow steady breathing; exhale as the hips settle forward, soften the belly on each inhale. Never bear down.
movements.regression: Stay low with hands resting on the front thigh (no arm sweep), reducing back-bend and balance demand; double-pad the back knee for sensitive knees.
movements.progression: Crescent variation: lift the back knee off the mat and reach the arms overhead for a deeper hip-flexor opening and light leg work (see crescent-lunge). Super-Sweaty flow pacing requires the Super Sweaty screening gate.

contraindications.why: Direct pressure on the kneecap on a hard surface is painful for many.
contraindications.substitution: Fold the mat or place a cushion under the back knee, or do a standing high-lunge version.

contraindications.flag: lower back (sensitive sacroiliac joint, common postpartum due to ligament laxity)
contraindications.why: Pushing the hips too far forward can compress the lower back and stress an already-loose SI joint.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the pelvis more upright, reduce the forward melt, and engage a gentle exhale-led lower-belly hug for support.

contraindications.why: Deep hip-flexor stretches can let the pelvis tip and the belly bulge forward if unsupported.
contraindications.substitution: Keep a tall pelvis, exhale gently as you move, and avoid letting the lower belly dome.

movements.intent: Opens the hip flexors that get short and grumpy from sitting, feeding, and rocking, easing the lower back. Preserves the existing intent and 'why' spirit exactly.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: low-lunge

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Kneeling hip-flexor stretch — high value for the postpartum anterior-hip tightness from feeding/rocking. Back-knee comfort (padding) is cued. As a held stretch it is diastasis-neutral and low-risk. Used as a flow transition in super_sweaty, not a long vigorous hold — appropriate.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Hip-flexor lengthening counters habitual shortening (general musculoskeletal reasoning); within ACOG low-impact postpartum ceiling.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Retain 'pad the back knee.' Avoid pushing the front knee past the toes. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Figure-Four Hug
movements.dose: Hold 50 s per side default (no_sweat: 60 s fully supported on the floor; slightly_sweaty: 50 s). Tempo still; this is a held stretch, not eligible for super_sweaty pacing. Rest 6 s transition between sides.
movements.setup: Lie on your back with both knees bent, feet on the floor; cross one ankle over the opposite thigh, just above the knee.
steps: Cross your ankle over the opposite thigh so your top shin makes a number four.
Thread your hands behind the back of your standing thigh.
Gently draw both legs in toward your chest until you feel the outer hip open.
Keep your head, shoulders, and tailbone heavy on the floor.
Softly flex the crossed foot to protect the knee.
veraCues: Ankle rests over the opposite knee
Hug the legs in gently
Head and shoulders stay heavy
Flex the crossed foot softly
movements.breath: Long, slow exhales to invite the hip to release; let each out-breath soften the grip a little more.
movements.regression: Seated figure-four in a chair: cross the ankle over the knee and hinge gently forward instead of hugging the legs in — accessible if lying down or deep hip flexion is uncomfortable.
movements.progression: Seated or lying with a deeper draw-in, or take the shape into a gentle supine twist afterward for the outer hip and glute. Stays within the postpartum ceiling (no impact, no loading).

contraindications.why: If the hip is tight, leverage can transfer into the crossed knee joint.
contraindications.substitution: Flex the top foot firmly and draw less aggressively, or use the seated chair version.

contraindications.why: Drawing unevenly can torque a loose postpartum SI joint.
contraindications.substitution: Keep both sides of the pelvis even and reduce the pull; use a strap behind the thigh instead of hands.

contraindications.why: Lifting both legs and curling the head up would load the abdominal wall.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the head down on the mat throughout; never crunch up to reach the leg — bring the hands behind the thigh instead.

movements.intent: Reaches deep into the outer hip that holds tension from carrying little ones on one side, easing the lower back. Preserves the existing 'sigh of relief' spirit.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: figure-four

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Supine outer-hip/glute stretch — fully passive, supported, no load, no impact. One of the safest hip openers. Diastasis-neutral.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Passive hip stretch within the postpartum ceiling; consistent with ACOG low-impact guidance.
vetting.modificationsRequired: None.

movements.name: Butterfly Stretch
movements.dose: Hold 45 s default (no_sweat: 60 s with support under the knees, fully restorative; slightly_sweaty: 45 s with a slow fold). Tempo still; not eligible for super_sweaty. Rest 6 s transition.
movements.setup: Sit tall, bring the soles of your feet together, and let your knees fall open to the sides. Bring the feet a comfortable distance from the body.
steps: Press the soles of your feet together and let your knees relax wide.
Hold your ankles or shins, sit tall through the crown of your head.
If you fold, hinge forward from the hips, leading with the chest, only as far as feels kind.
Keep the spine long rather than rounding hard.
Let gravity, not force, open the inner thighs.
veraCues: Soles together, knees fall wide
Fold only as far as feels kind
Long spine, soft shoulders
Let gravity do the work
movements.breath: Easy, unhurried breath; soften the inner thighs a touch more on each exhale. No forcing.
movements.regression: Sit up on a folded blanket or cushion to lift the hips, and place rolled supports under each knee — turns it into a passive, fully supported opener for tight hips or sensitive knees.
movements.progression: Reclined butterfly (supta baddha konasana) for a longer restorative hold, or a slightly deeper hip-led forward fold keeping the spine long. Stays low-impact and within the postpartum ceiling.

contraindications.why: Letting the knees drop with no support can strain the inner knee in hypermobile or sensitive joints.
contraindications.substitution: Place firm support under each thigh so the knees are held rather than hanging.

contraindications.why: Rounding hard to fold forward can aggravate a sensitive lower back.
contraindications.substitution: Sit elevated on a cushion and fold only from the hips with a long spine, or skip the fold entirely.

contraindications.why: A forced deep forward fold can increase intra-abdominal pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the fold gentle, exhale as you hinge, and stop before any belly bulging.

movements.intent: Softens the inner thighs and hips and invites them to let go, at the unhurried pace the body deserves. Preserves the existing spirit exactly.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: butterfly

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Seated inner-thigh/hip stretch, supported, passive. Pelvic relaxin-related laxity means the inner-thigh/adductor range may be larger than expected — cue 'only as far as feels kind' (present) to avoid overstretch. No load, no impact.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Passive hip stretch within the postpartum ceiling; consistent with ACOG low-impact guidance.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Keep knee support option (cued in no_sweat). Avoid forcing the fold. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Seated Twist
movements.dose: Hold 45 s per side default (no_sweat: 50 s gentle and supported; slightly_sweaty: 45 s). Tempo slow, breath-led — twist a little further on each exhale. Not eligible for super_sweaty. Rest 6 s transition between sides.
movements.setup: Sit tall, either cross-legged or with legs extended; place one hand behind you and the other on the opposite thigh or knee.
steps: Grow tall through the crown of your head first, lifting out of your hips.
On an exhale, turn slowly from your middle back toward one side.
Let the back hand rest lightly behind you for support, not to crank.
Keep the chin in line with the breastbone so the neck follows the spine.
Let your eyes travel last, softly over the back shoulder.
veraCues: Grow tall first, then turn
Twist from your middle, not your neck
Let your eyes follow slowly
Each exhale, a little more ease
movements.breath: Inhale to lengthen the spine, exhale to rotate a touch further. Keep the breath moving — never hold it in the twist.
movements.regression: Chair seated twist: sit on a sturdy chair, turn to hold the chair back gently. Same spinal rotation with full support and no floor sitting — friendly for stiff hips.
movements.progression: A slightly deeper open twist keeping length in the spine, or pair with a gentle thread-the-needle for the upper back. Stays gentle and within the postpartum ceiling — twists stay soft and open, never wrenched.

contraindications.flag: lower back (post-cesarean or recent abdominal healing)
contraindications.why: Deep rotation can pull on healing abdominal or incision tissue.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the twist shallow and open (rotating away from the closed side), and let the breath, not the arm, lead.

contraindications.why: Forced or end-range twisting can load the linea alba and pelvic floor.
contraindications.substitution: Twist gently to a comfortable range, exhale into the turn, and keep the belly soft rather than gripped.

contraindications.why: Leading the twist with the head can strain the neck.
