contraindications.substitution: Initiate from the mid-back and let the head turn last and least.

movements.intent: Keeps the spine turning freely for everyday checking-the-back-seat moves and eases the stiff, sat-too-long feeling in the middle back. Preserves the existing intent. Re-tagged conceptually from mobility to yoga while keeping the same blocks.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: seated-twist

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Gentle seated rotation, breath-led, twist-on-exhale. Rotation is diastasis-safe when soft and open. The source's 'never wrenched' boundary is correct. No load, no impact.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Gentle controlled spinal rotation appropriate postpartum when kept breath-led (pelvic health physiotherapy consensus; ACOG gradual progression).
vetting.modificationsRequired: Retain 'twist from the mid-back, grow tall first, never force it.' Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Thread the Needle
movements.dose: Hold 50 s per side default (no_sweat: 60 s settled; slightly_sweaty: 50 s). Tempo still, breath-led. Not eligible for super_sweaty. Rest 6 s transition between sides.
movements.setup: Begin on hands and knees, knees under hips, hands under shoulders.
steps: From all fours, reach one arm up toward the ceiling to open the chest.
Thread that arm underneath your body, palm facing up, sliding it across the mat.
Rest the ear and shoulder gently down on the mat.
Keep your hips high and stacked over your knees.
Let the top arm reach forward or rest on your lower back, whichever is kinder.
veraCues: Slide the arm under, palm up
Rest your ear and shoulder down
Hips stay high and steady
Breathe into the back of your shoulder
movements.breath: Slow breaths directed into the back of the down shoulder; exhales to release the upper-back tension.
movements.regression: Seated or standing wall version: cross one arm across the body and use the other to draw it gently — same upper-back and shoulder opening without floor weight on the shoulder or neck.
movements.progression: Add a gentle bind by reaching the top arm behind the back, or hold longer for a deeper thoracic release. Stays low-impact and within the postpartum ceiling.

contraindications.flag: shoulders / neck (bearing weight on the down shoulder)
contraindications.why: Resting the shoulder and ear on the floor loads the neck and shoulder, which can pinch an irritable joint.
contraindications.substitution: Use the seated cross-body regression so no weight rests on the neck or shoulder.

contraindications.why: Tabletop loads the supporting wrist, a tender spot postpartum.
contraindications.substitution: Lower onto the supporting forearm, or use the standing/seated version.

contraindications.why: Letting the hips drift or twist unevenly can torque the lower back.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the hips high and square over the knees, and reduce the depth of the thread.

movements.intent: Unwinds the upper back and shoulders where baby-carrying tension sets up camp — a gentle twist that meets you where it is tight. Preserves the existing intent exactly.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: thread-needle

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Quadruped thoracic twist with the shoulder resting down — gentle, supported, low-load. Minor neck/shoulder positional comfort and wrist weight-bearing on the supporting arm. Diastasis-neutral.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Gentle thoracic rotation within the postpartum ceiling; general mobility reasoning.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Keep wrist-prep available for the supporting arm. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Rag-Doll Fold
movements.dose: Hold 45 s default (no_sweat: 60 s hanging heavy, fully passive; slightly_sweaty: 45 s with gentle sway). Tempo slow. Not eligible for super_sweaty as a held release. Rest 6 s transition.
movements.setup: Stand with feet hip-width apart, knees generously bent.
steps: Soften your knees a lot, then hinge forward from the hips and let your upper body hang.
Let your head, neck, and arms be completely heavy.
Hold opposite elbows and let the weight of your arms gently lengthen your spine.
Nod your head 'yes' and shake it 'no' to release the neck.
To come up, bend your knees more and roll up slowly, head last.
veraCues: Knees soft, very soft
Hang heavy like a rag doll
Nod yes, shake no
Sway a little if it feels nice
movements.breath: Easy belly breathing while hanging; long exhales to deepen the release. Rise slowly to avoid lightheadedness.
movements.regression: Half fold with hands on a wall or chair seat, back flat and hips hinging — same back-line lengthening with no head-below-heart position (kinder for hypertension and dizziness).
movements.progression: A longer fold with straighter (never locked) legs for more hamstring length, or a slow ragdoll sway. Stays low-impact within the postpartum ceiling; rise slowly to manage blood pressure.

contraindications.why: A loaded rounded forward bend can aggravate a sensitive lumbar spine.
contraindications.substitution: Keep knees very bent, or use the half-fold-at-the-wall regression with a flat back.

contraindications.why: Head below heart can cause a blood-pressure drop and lightheadedness; rising too fast worsens it.
contraindications.substitution: Use the supported half-fold and rise slowly with bent knees, or skip the inversion.

contraindications.why: Deep flexion plus a hard exhale can increase downward pressure on the pelvic floor.
contraindications.substitution: Keep knees bent, fold gently, and avoid straining; exhale softly rather than forcing the fold.

movements.intent: Lets the whole back of the body hang and lengthen with zero effort — a heavy, swingy release for the spine after a long day. Preserves the existing intent exactly.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: forward-fold

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Standing rag-doll hang — passive posterior-chain release. The one real flag is ORTHOSTATIC: rising from a full forward fold drops then spikes cerebral perfusion and can cause light-headedness/fainting, more likely postpartum (anaemia, deconditioning, low BP). Knees-soft is cued, which is good. No load, no impact.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Passive posterior-chain stretch; orthostatic-hypotension caution on rising from a fold (mechanism well established) [evidence: postpartum-specific magnitude thin — flag].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Make 'rise slowly, roll up bone by bone, let your head come up last' an explicit spoken cue (the source notes this in prog — promote it to a cue). 'If you feel light-headed, pause halfway.' Keep knees soft.

movements.name: Gentle Sunrise Flow
movements.dose: no_sweat: move through once per side slowly, ~60 s, pausing 2-3 breaths in each shape. slightly_sweaty: 2 rounds per side linking breath to movement, ~75 s. super_sweaty: 3-4 continuous rounds per side at a steady flowing pace to build heart rate (requires Super Sweaty screening gate). One breath per movement. Rest 6 s transition.
movements.setup: Stand tall at the front of your mat, feet hip-width, arms by your sides.
steps: Inhale, sweep your arms wide and up overhead (reuses arm-sweeps shape).
Exhale, soften your knees and fold forward into a rag-doll fold.
Inhale, step one foot back into a low lunge, back knee down.
Exhale, press back to hands-and-knees, then up and back to downward dog (or stay in tabletop).
Inhale, lower to a baby cobra lift; exhale, press back to child's pose to rest.
Step forward and slowly roll up to standing to begin the next breath cycle.
veraCues: Inhale arms up, exhale fold soft
One breath, one movement, no rush
Bend your knees whenever you like
Let your breath lead the whole flow
movements.breath: One breath per movement, the heartbeat of the flow: inhale to open and lengthen, exhale to fold and ground. Never hold the breath; if the breath gets choppy, slow down.
movements.regression: Drop the downward dog and stay in tabletop; replace the rag-doll fold with a half-fold at a chair. Keeps the breath-led flow accessible with no inversion and no wrist load.
movements.progression: Add the crescent variation (back knee lifted) in the lunge and flow continuously for the Super-Sweaty pacing. Higher heart rate while staying low-impact and jump-free. Super-Sweaty pacing requires the Super Sweaty screening gate.

contraindications.why: The down-dog / tabletop step loads the wrists repeatedly.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the flow standing: arms-up, fold, gentle standing back-bend, repeat — omitting the floor portion.

contraindications.why: Repeated up-and-down between standing and folding moves the head above and below the heart.
contraindications.substitution: Slow the transitions, keep the head higher (half-fold), and rise gradually.

contraindications.why: Faster flowing and repeated transitions can lead to breath-holding and downward pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Stay at the no_sweat tempo, exhale on every fold and lift, and keep the lower belly from bulging.

movements.intent: A flowing, breath-linked warm-up-into-main sequence that wakes the whole body, links movement to breath, and scales from a restorative wake-up to a gentle low-impact cardio flow. A flowing alternative to the existing static warm-up moves.
movements.posesNote: new keyframe rig OR reuse existing rigs (arm-sweeps, forward-fold, low-lunge, down-dog, baby-cobra, childs-pose) chained as a sequence — engineering may implement as a sequence-of-existing-rigs item to avoid authoring a new rig. Justification: every constituent shape already has a hand-authored rig, so honest reuse is possible; a true new rig is only needed if the engine cannot chain existing rigs.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Breath-linked flow scaling from restorative to low-impact cardio. Flags depend on the shapes inside it: likely includes forward fold (orthostatic), possibly down-dog (wrist/inversion), and lunges (knee). The super_sweaty continuous-round pacing raises HR appropriately and is gated. No jumping if it stays a flow.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Low-impact flowing aerobic/mobility work consistent with ACOG postpartum guidance; super-sweaty pacing gated by the screening gate.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Inherit the per-shape mitigations: 'rise slowly from any fold' (orthostatic), wrist-prep if down-dog is included, knee-tracking in lunges. Confirm no shape introduces a jump or a floor plank. Keep the super_sweaty screening gate. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Crescent Lunge
movements.dose: slightly_sweaty: hold 40 s per side, arms up, breathing steadily. super_sweaty: add 4-6 slow pulses (1-2 inches) before holding, 45 s per side, to build leg work and heart rate (requires Super Sweaty screening gate). Tempo controlled. Rest 6 s transition between sides.
movements.setup: From a low lunge, tuck the back toes and lift the back knee off the mat so the back leg is long and strong.
steps: From your low lunge, press into the back toes and lift the back knee off the mat.
Stack your front knee over your front ankle, back leg long and active.
On an inhale, sweep your arms up alongside your ears.
Draw your lower ribs down gently so your back does not arch hard.
Hold tall and steady, gazing softly forward.
veraCues: Lift the back knee, leg long and strong
Front knee over your ankle
Reach your arms up, ribs soft
Stand tall and breathe steady
movements.breath: Inhale to reach and lengthen, exhale to soften the ribs and settle. Steady breath throughout — exhale on any pulse or exertion.
movements.regression: Drop the back knee to the mat (this is the existing low-lunge): removes the balance and leg-strength demand and the lower-back load. Hands can rest on the front thigh instead of reaching up.
movements.progression: Add slow pulses or a gentle back-bend reach for the Super-Sweaty pacing. Stays low-impact, no jumping. Super-Sweaty pacing requires the Super Sweaty screening gate.

contraindications.why: Reaching the arms overhead in a lunge invites the lower back to arch and the loose SI joint to strain.
contraindications.substitution: Drop to the low-lunge regression, keep hands on the front thigh, and draw the lower ribs down.

contraindications.why: An overhead arms-up back-bend position can let the belly dome and increase downward pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Keep arms lower (shoulder height or on the hips), exhale on the lift, and keep the lower belly from bulging.

contraindications.why: Letting the front knee drift past the toes or collapse inward stresses the joint.
contraindications.substitution: Stack the knee over the ankle and track it over the second toe; shorten the stance if needed.

movements.intent: A standing, energized hip-flexor opener and leg-strengthener that adds heat and balance to the main block while staying jump-free and low-impact — the active counterpart to the restorative low-lunge.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig low-lunge (with back knee lifted) — honest partial reuse: the front-knee-bent, arms-up silhouette is shared; the lifted back leg differs, so the rig should be adapted from low-lunge keyframes rather than authored from scratch. Caption-only fallback acceptable if rig adaptation is not feasible.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Standing high-lunge hip-flexor opener and leg strengthener, back knee lifted, arms overhead. Flags: balance (fall), front-knee tracking, and overhead arms add mild BP/shoulder demand. Pulses (super_sweaty) increase leg work and HR without impact — gated. No jumping.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Standing lunge strength/mobility per ACSM and general biomechanics; low-impact, jump-free; super-sweaty pacing gated by the screening gate.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Cue 'front knee over ankle' and offer light support. 'Breathe steadily' for the arms-overhead hold. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Gentle Half-Pigeon
movements.dose: Hold 60 s per side default (no_sweat: 75 s fully supported with props, restorative; slightly_sweaty: 60 s). Tempo still, breath-led. Not eligible for super_sweaty. Rest 6 s transition between sides.
movements.setup: From hands and knees or downward dog, bring one knee forward toward the same-side wrist; the shin angles across the mat (steeper if tighter). Extend the other leg long behind you.
steps: Bring the front knee forward and let the shin angle across the mat at a comfortable angle.
Slide the back leg long behind you, top of the foot resting down.
Square your hips toward the front of the mat as best you can.
Place a cushion or folded blanket under the front hip so you are not dumping into it.
Stay upright on your hands, or walk them forward to fold gently over the front shin.
veraCues: Front shin in, support under your hip
Square your hips, no forcing
Stay tall or melt forward, your choice
Let the outer hip slowly soften
movements.breath: Long, slow exhales to coax the deep hip open; let each out-breath release a little more. Never push into pain.
movements.regression: Reclined figure-four (the existing figure-four-hug, lying on the back) gives the same outer-hip and glute opening with full support, no front-knee load, and no weight-bearing — the recommended default for sensitive knees or early postpartum.
movements.progression: A deeper forward fold over the front shin, or a longer restorative hold with the chest down on a bolster. Stays low-impact and within the postpartum ceiling.

contraindications.why: Half-pigeon places real rotational load on the front knee; this is the pose's main risk and it is significant.
contraindications.substitution: Use the reclined figure-four regression entirely, which removes all knee load; keep the front foot flexed if attempting pigeon.

contraindications.flag: lower back / sacroiliac joint (postpartum ligament laxity)
contraindications.why: Uneven hips and a loose SI joint can be strained by the asymmetrical position.
contraindications.substitution: Support the front hip well with a cushion to keep the pelvis level, or use the reclined figure-four.

contraindications.why: Folding deeply forward can increase intra-abdominal pressure if the breath is held.
contraindications.substitution: Stay more upright, exhale as you fold, and keep the lower belly soft.

movements.intent: A deep but optional outer-hip and glute release for stubborn carrying-side tension, offered with strong props and a fully supported regression because the front-knee load makes it the riskiest hip opener in the bank.
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback. Justification: half-pigeon is a genuinely distinct prone shape with no existing rig; the closest rig (figure-four) is supine and would misrepresent the pose. Authoring a new rig is costly, and the move ships with a strong reclined-figure-four regression, so a captioned cue-led version is the honest, lower-cost choice until a rig is funded.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: The source itself flags this as the RISKIEST hip opener in the bank because of front-KNEE load in a flexed/rotated position — postpartum relaxin-related ligament laxity makes the medial knee and the front-shin position genuinely vulnerable. It is a deep passive stretch (no_sweat/slightly only, correctly never super_sweaty). Pelvic/SI discomfort is also possible.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Deep hip-opener with front-knee load; knee-protection caution is standard yoga-therapy/physiotherapy practice [evidence: thin on a named source — flag]; kept restorative within the postpartum ceiling.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Make the strongly-supported/figure-four regression the DEFAULT first offering, not an alternative — i.e. lead with supine figure-four and only offer floor half-pigeon with props for those without knee discomfort. Add 'if you feel anything in the front knee, come out and use the figure-four version.' Keep props (bolster under the hip) mandatory in the cue. Confirm never super_sweaty (correct).

movements.name: Standing Forward Fold Variations
movements.dose: Hold 45 s default cycling through 2-3 variations (no_sweat: 60 s, supported half-fold, fully passive; slightly_sweaty: 45 s including a wide-legged variation). Tempo slow. Not eligible for super_sweaty as a held release. Rest 6 s transition.
movements.setup: Stand with feet hip-width (or wider for the wide-legged variation), knees softly bent.
steps: Begin with a half-fold: hands on a wall or chair, hips back, back long and flat.
Then soften the knees and fold all the way down, letting the head hang (rag-doll).
For the wide-legged variation, step the feet wide and fold between them, hands to the floor or a block.
Optionally clasp the hands behind the back for a gentle shoulder opener as you fold.
To rise, bend the knees and roll up slowly, head coming up last.
veraCues: Half-fold first, back long and flat
Soft knees, then hang heavy
Step wide and fold between your feet
Roll up slow, head comes up last
movements.breath: Easy breath throughout; long exhales in each fold. Rise slowly to avoid lightheadedness.
movements.regression: Stay in the supported half-fold at the wall or a chair the whole time — flat back, hips hinging, head level with the heart. Friendly for hypertension, dizziness, and sensitive lower backs.
movements.progression: Deeper full folds with straighter (never locked) legs, or the bound-shoulder wide-legged fold. Stays low-impact within the postpartum ceiling; rise slowly to manage blood pressure.

contraindications.why: Repeated head-below-heart folds can drop blood pressure and cause lightheadedness.
contraindications.substitution: Use only the supported half-fold at the wall and rise slowly with bent knees.

contraindications.why: Deep rounded loaded flexion can aggravate a sensitive lumbar spine.
contraindications.substitution: Keep knees generously bent or stay in the flat-back half-fold; avoid the deepest folds.

contraindications.why: Deep flexion with a forced exhale increases downward pressure on the pelvic floor.
contraindications.substitution: Fold gently with bent knees, exhale softly, and stop before any belly bulging.

movements.intent: A small family of forward-fold options spanning supported, classic, and wide-legged so the same posterior-chain release scales across warm-up, main, and wind-down and across mobility levels. Complements the existing rag-doll fold with safer, more graded entry points.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig forward-fold. Justification: the core silhouette is the existing rag-doll fold; variations are conveyed through captions and cues, so no new rig is needed for the primary shape. Caption-only for the wide-legged variation.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: A family of forward folds (supported half-fold, classic, wide-legged). Same orthostatic-on-rising flag as forward-fold, plus the wide-legged variation adds an adductor stretch where postpartum laxity warrants caution. Passive, no load, no impact.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Passive posterior-chain/adductor stretch within the postpartum ceiling; orthostatic caution on rising (mechanism well established) [evidence: postpartum magnitude thin — flag].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Promote 'rise slowly, head up last' to a spoken cue (orthostatic). Cap the wide-legged adductor range ('only as far as feels kind'). Keep knees soft/never locked (stated). Default the supported half-fold for less mobile users. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Cobra to Child's Flow
movements.dose: no_sweat: 4-5 slow rounds, ~60 s, pausing to rest in child's pose. slightly_sweaty: 6-8 rounds linking breath to movement, ~75 s. Tempo slow and wave-like; one breath per movement. Not eligible for super_sweaty (gentle spinal flow, not cardio). Rest 6 s transition.
movements.setup: Lie face down with legs long, hands under the shoulders, elbows hugging in. Forehead resting down.
steps: Inhale, press lightly through the hands and lift your chest into a low baby cobra.
Keep the lift small and led by the upper back, shoulders sliding away from the ears.
Exhale, press back through hands-and-knees and sink the hips toward your heels into child's pose.
Rest your forehead down and let the back round and breathe.
Inhale to glide forward and rise into the next gentle cobra; flow with the breath.
veraCues: Inhale, lift your chest just a little
Low is lovely, lead with your upper back
Exhale, melt back to child's pose
Move like a slow wave with your breath
movements.breath: One breath per movement: inhale to open the chest and lift, exhale to fold back and rest. Soft breath, never held; exhale on the lift.
movements.regression: Sphinx-to-child's: rest on the forearms instead of the hands for the back-bend (less wrist load and a gentler lift), then settle back to child's pose. Keeps the soothing flow with less load.
movements.progression: A slightly higher (still low) cobra lift or a longer, slower flow for a deeper upper-back opener. Stays gentle and within the postpartum ceiling — never a full upward dog or deep back-bend.

contraindications.why: Pressing up from cobra and moving to all-fours loads the wrists repeatedly.
contraindications.substitution: Use the sphinx-on-forearms regression so the wrists are not extended under load.

contraindications.why: Lifting too high in the back-bend compresses the lower back.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the cobra low and upper-back-led; engage a gentle exhale and lower-belly support on the lift.

contraindications.why: Back-bends stretch the abdominal wall and can encourage the belly to dome along the midline.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the lift low, exhale as you lift, and watch for / avoid any doming or coning along the belly midline; reduce range if it appears.

movements.intent: A soothing breath-linked spinal flow that counters the forward-curl of feeding and carrying by gently opening the chest, then resting the back — pairing the existing baby-cobra and child's-pose into one wave. Diastasis-safe by keeping the back-bend low and exhale-led.
movements.posesNote: reuse rigs baby-cobra and childs-pose, chained as a two-pose sequence. Justification: both shapes already have hand-authored rigs (baby-cobra at poses.js line 1658, childs-pose at 3167), so the flow needs no new rig — only sequence wiring. Caption-only fallback if the engine cannot chain two rigs.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Breath-linked spinal flow pairing baby-cobra (back extension over a stretched abdomen) with child's pose (flexion/rest). Inherits the baby-cobra diastasis caveat: the cobra portion must stay LOW and exhale-led so the linea alba is not over-stretched. The source correctly keeps it gentle and excludes super_sweaty. Wrist load in the cobra-to-childs transition is minor. Child's pose is fully restorative.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Gentle spinal flow; cobra kept low and exhale-led respects diastasis caution (ACOG postpartum guidance; pelvic health physiotherapy consensus).
vetting.modificationsRequired: Carry the baby-cobra cap: 'keep the cobra low, ribs heavy, lead with the upper back.' Add a note for symptomatic/early diastasis to keep the lift minimal. Confirm it never becomes upward-dog or a deep back-bend (prog states this).

movements.name: Warrior I
movements.dose: slightly_sweaty: hold 45 s per side, arms reaching up, breathing steadily. super_sweaty: add 4-6 slow controlled pulses before holding, 50 s per side, to build leg work (requires Super Sweaty screening gate). Tempo controlled. Rest 6 s transition between sides.
movements.setup: From standing, step one foot back and turn the back foot to about 45 degrees, heel grounded. Square the hips toward the front of the mat.
steps: Step one foot back and angle the back foot down at about forty-five degrees, heel pressing into the floor.
Bend the front knee so it stacks over the front ankle.
Draw the back hip forward so both hip points face the front of the mat.
On an inhale, sweep the arms up alongside the ears, shoulders relaxed.
Lift the chest, soften the lower ribs, and hold tall and strong.
veraCues: Front knee over your ankle, back heel down
Hug your back hip forward
Sweep your arms up, shoulders soft
Stand strong and proud, keep breathing
movements.breath: Inhale to reach and lengthen, exhale to ground through the feet and settle. Steady breath; exhale on any pulse or exertion.
movements.regression: Shorten the stance and rest the hands on the hips instead of reaching overhead, or take a high-lunge version with hands on the front thigh — less balance, lower-back, and shoulder demand.
movements.progression: Add slow controlled pulses or flow between Warrior I and the gentle sunrise flow for the Super-Sweaty pacing. Stays low-impact, no jumping. Super-Sweaty pacing requires the Super Sweaty screening gate.

contraindications.why: Squaring the hips with arms overhead invites the lower back to arch and can strain a loose SI joint.
contraindications.substitution: Hands to hips, shorten the stance, and draw the lower ribs down; reduce the back-bend.

contraindications.why: Sustained overhead arm reach can aggravate an irritable shoulder and is sometimes cautioned with high blood pressure. [evidence: thin — flag] Clinical reasoning: keep arms lower if either applies.
contraindications.substitution: Bring the arms to shoulder height, cactus arms, or rest them on the hips.

contraindications.why: Letting the front knee drift past the toes or collapse inward stresses the joint.
contraindications.substitution: Stack the knee over the ankle, track it over the second toe, and shorten the stance if needed.

contraindications.why: An overhead arms-up position can let the belly dome and add downward pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Keep arms lower, exhale on the lift, and keep the lower belly from bulging.

movements.intent: A grounded, powerful standing strength-and-stability pose that builds leg strength and confidence, complementing the existing Warrior II with a square-hipped, forward-facing shape. Adds heat to the main block while staying jump-free and low-impact.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig warrior2 (legs/stance) with adapted arms. Honest partial reuse: the bent-front-knee stance is shared with Warrior II (rig at poses.js line 1157); the arms differ (overhead/forward vs wide), so arms should be adapted from the warrior2 keyframes. Caption-only fallback acceptable if arm adaptation is not feasible.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Square-hipped standing strength/stability hold, arms overhead. Flags: front-knee tracking, the squared-hip position can stress the back-leg SI joint/hip in laxer postpartum bodies, and overhead arms add mild BP/shoulder demand. Pulses (super_sweaty) build leg work without impact — gated. No jumping.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Standing strength pose per ACSM strength reasoning; low-impact, jump-free; super-sweaty pacing gated by the screening gate.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Cue 'front knee over ankle,' allow a slightly shorter stance / hands-to-hips option if the squared hips pinch, and 'breathe steadily' for the overhead hold. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Child's Pose
movements.dose: 55-90s hold; 1 round. No Sweat / Meditation: 75-90s with long slow breaths. Slightly Sweaty: 55-70s. Super Sweaty: 60-75s as a deliberate down-regulation closer. Tempo: still. Rest: none (this IS the rest).
movements.setup: Kneel on a mat or rug. Bring big toes together and let knees travel as wide as feels kind. Sink hips back toward heels and walk hands forward, forehead resting on the mat, a block, or stacked fists.
steps: Kneel and bring big toes to touch, knees as wide as comfortable.
Sit your hips back toward your heels, letting them travel only as far as feels easy.
Walk your hands forward and let your forehead come to rest.
Let your arms be long and heavy, shoulders melting away from ears.
Breathe slowly into the back of your ribs and stay.
To come up, walk hands back and rise one slow breath at a time.
veraCues: Hips sink back toward your heels
Arms long, forehead heavy
Breathe wide into your back
Take your knees as wide as feels good
movements.breath: Slow, even nasal breathing directed into the back ribs and low back. Let the exhale be unhurried and slightly longer than the inhale to invite the nervous system to settle. No bearing down.
movements.regression: Place a folded blanket or cushion under your hips (between calves and thighs) and another under your forehead so the chest is supported; or take a wide-knee version to make room for the belly. If kneeling is uncomfortable, do a seated forward rest with forearms on a table instead.
movements.progression: Lengthen the hold and walk the hands a little further forward for more side-body and shoulder opening, keeping the forehead supported. This stays well within the postpartum ceiling — it is a deeper rest, never more intense.

contraindications.why: Deep knee flexion and direct pressure on the kneecaps can aggravate sensitive or injured knees.
contraindications.substitution: Pad under the knees and shins with a folded blanket, sit on a cushion to reduce the bend, or use the seated forearms-on-table rest.

contraindications.why: Most lower backs love this shape, but rounding under load can occasionally irritate an acute disc issue.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce the fold, support the chest on a bolster so the spine stays long, or substitute Legs-Up Rest.

contraindications.why: With knees together the belly is compressed against the thighs, which can feel like pressure on a healing core.
contraindications.substitution: Take the wide-knee variation so the belly hangs into open space; keep the breath soft with no bearing down.

contraindications.why: Head-below-heart folds and deep abdominal compression may not suit uncontrolled high blood pressure or the very early postpartum window.
contraindications.substitution: Prop the chest and head higher than the hips on bolsters, or choose a seated, upright breath instead; clear return to exercise with your provider per ACOG postpartum guidance.

movements.intent: A full-permission rest that lengthens the lower back and hips and signals the body it is safe to soften — the gentle landing that ends a session. Gentle spinal flexion and quiet, breath-led rest support relaxation after activity, consistent with ACOG postpartum guidance favouring gradual, low-impact movement [evidence: thin on the specific relaxation claim — flag; clinical reasoning: parasympathetic tone rises with slow extended exhalation and supported stillness].
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: childs-pose

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Restorative kneeling rest with gentle spinal flexion. Knees-wide option accommodates a postpartum belly and reduces knee/ankle strain. No exertional, BP, or core-pressure concern — it IS the down-regulation. Knee/ankle comfort is the only minor flag.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Restorative rest consistent with ACOG postpartum guidance favouring gradual low-impact movement [evidence: thin on the specific relaxation claim — flag; parasympathetic tone rises with slow exhalation and supported stillness].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Retain knees-wide and forehead-supported options. Offer a cushion under the hips/ankles for comfort. Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Happy Baby
movements.dose: 45-70s; 1 round. No Sweat: 60-70s with slow side-to-side rocking. Slightly Sweaty: 45-60s. Super Sweaty: 50-65s as a hip-release closer. Tempo: slow gentle rocking or still. Rest: none.
movements.setup: Lie on your back on a mat. Draw both knees toward your chest, then guide the knees out toward the armpits. Reach for the outer feet, ankles, or shins — whatever your hands reach comfortably with the tailbone heavy.
steps: Lie on your back and hug both knees in toward your chest.
Let the knees drift apart, drawing them toward your armpits.
Reach for the outer edges of your feet, your ankles, or your shins.
Flex your feet gently so soles face the ceiling, shins roughly vertical.
Keep your tailbone and lower back heavy on the mat.
Rock slowly side to side if it feels soothing, then release feet to the floor.
veraCues: Knees drift toward your armpits
Hold shins or feet, whatever reaches
Rock side to side, slow and small
Keep your tailbone heavy
movements.breath: Easy, continuous breathing into the belly and back. Let each exhale soften the inner thighs and lower back a little more. No straining to reach the feet and no breath-holding.
movements.regression: Hold the backs of the thighs instead of the feet, or use a yoga strap or scarf looped around each foot. Keep one foot on the floor at a time if reaching both feels like too much for the lower back.
movements.progression: Hold the outer feet with arms inside the knees and gently widen the knees further for a deeper inner-thigh and hip release, keeping the tailbone grounded. Remains a passive opener — no added load.

contraindications.why: If the tailbone lifts and the lower back rounds hard off the floor, this can strain a sensitive low back.
contraindications.substitution: Hold the thighs higher up, keep the tailbone down, or do one leg at a time; substitute Figure-Four Hug if needed.

contraindications.why: Deep hip and knee flexion can pinch in tight or irritated hip joints or sensitive knees.
contraindications.substitution: Use a strap around the feet to keep arms long and reduce the depth, and widen the knees only as far as is comfortable.

contraindications.why: Reaching for both feet at once can tempt a breath-hold and abdominal bracing, which raises intra-abdominal pressure on a healing core.
contraindications.substitution: Use straps, keep the breath flowing, and avoid crunching the head and shoulders up to reach — head stays down.

contraindications.why: A supine position with legs elevated is generally fine, but avoid breath-holding while reaching.
contraindications.substitution: Keep breathing freely throughout; if lying flat is uncomfortable, prop the head on a thin cushion.

movements.intent: A lying-down hip and lower-back opener that asks for very little while releasing a tired pelvis and spine; the gentle rocking is genuinely soothing. Passive, supported hip mobility suits the postpartum body's preference for low-impact, gradual loading per ACOG postpartum activity guidance.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: happy-baby

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Supine passive hip/low-back opener with gentle rocking. No load, no impact, tailbone grounded — diastasis-neutral and pelvic-floor-gentle. Widening the knees is a passive range increase only.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Passive supported hip mobility within the postpartum ceiling (ACOG postpartum activity guidance).
vetting.modificationsRequired: None. Keep 'tailbone heavy' cue to avoid over-tucking.

movements.name: Legs-Up Rest
movements.dose: 55-120s; 1 round. No Sweat / Meditation: 90-120s of pure rest. Slightly Sweaty: 60-75s. Super Sweaty: 75-120s — the single best down-regulation closer after vigorous work. Tempo: still. Rest: none.
movements.setup: Lie on your back near a wall or sofa. Send the legs up the wall, or float them toward the ceiling with a slight knee bend, or rest calves on a chair seat. Let the arms fall open, palms up.
steps: Lie on your back and bring your hips close to a wall or the front of a chair.
Send your legs up the wall, or rest your calves on the chair seat.
If you have no wall, simply float the legs up with knees softly bent.
Let your arms rest wide and heavy, palms turned up.
Soften your jaw, your shoulders, and the space between your eyebrows.
Stay and breathe; to finish, bend the knees and roll gently to one side.
veraCues: Legs float up, easy and tall
Arms wide and heavy
Soften your jaw
Nothing to do here, just breathe
movements.breath: Quiet, natural breathing — no technique required. If you wish, let the exhale lengthen so it gently outlasts the inhale to deepen the rest. Let the breath move on its own.
movements.regression: Rest calves on a chair or sofa seat (90-90) instead of straight up the wall — easier on the hamstrings and lower back. A cushion under the hips is optional, not required.
movements.progression: Move closer to the wall for a fuller hamstring lengthening and stay longer, or add the longer guided breath here. This stays restorative by design — there is no 'harder' version that adds intensity, only more stillness.

contraindications.why: Hips jammed too close to the wall with very straight legs can tug the lower back.
contraindications.substitution: Slide the hips a few inches from the wall, soften the knees, or use the chair-seat version.

contraindications.why: Legs-elevated inversions are mild, but those with poorly controlled high blood pressure may feel head pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Use the lower chair-seat version with legs only slightly above the heart, and keep the head supported.

contraindications.why: Generally very safe and even pressure-relieving, but lifting both straight legs from the floor to get up can strain a healing core.
contraindications.substitution: Always enter and exit by rolling to your side and using your hands, never by lifting both straight legs against gravity.

contraindications.flag: first-trimester-supine or very recent birth caution
contraindications.why: Prolonged flat-on-back time is sometimes limited later in pregnancy; the very early postpartum window calls for provider clearance.
contraindications.substitution: Prop the upper body on a cushion at a slight incline; confirm return-to-exercise timing with your provider per ACOG guidance.

movements.intent: A do-nothing restorative rest that lets tired, heavy legs lighten and invites the whole system to downshift — the kindest possible close, especially after Super Sweaty effort. Supported elevation and stillness are a recognised restorative practice; the calming effect aligns with slow-breathing parasympathetic principles [evidence: physiological mechanism well established for slow breathing; specific 'legs-up' clinical trials are thin — flag].
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: legs-up

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Mild restorative inversion (legs up). Two minor flags: legs-elevated supine can transiently raise central venous return — generally benign but worth a gentle note for anyone with cardiovascular concerns; and there is no 'harder' version, which is exactly right for an anti-compulsion close. No core/pelvic-floor load.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Restorative practice; calming effect aligns with slow-breathing parasympathetic principles [evidence: mechanism well established for slow breathing; legs-up-specific trials thin — flag].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Optional gentle note that anyone who feels uncomfortable can lower the legs or bend the knees. Otherwise approved; correctly restorative-only across all tiers.

movements.name: Box Breathing
movements.dose: 75-120s; equal counts of 4 (in-hold-out-hold). No Sweat / Meditation: extend to 90-120s, optionally lengthening counts to 5. Slightly Sweaty: 75-90s. Super Sweaty: 90-120s to actively bring the heart rate and breath back down. Tempo: 4-4-4-4 metered. Rest: this is the rest.
movements.setup: Sit comfortably — cross-legged, on a chair with feet flat, or propped against a wall. Let the spine be tall but easy, hands resting on the thighs or belly, shoulders soft.
steps: Settle into a comfortable upright seat and let your shoulders drop.
Breathe out fully and easily to begin empty.
Breathe in through the nose for a slow count of four.
Hold the breath gently for four — no clenching, no gripping.
Breathe out for four, soft and complete.
Rest empty for four, then begin the next round; repeat for the set.
veraCues: Breathe in for four
Hold gently for four
Breathe out for four
Rest empty for four, nice and easy
movements.breath: Equal-ratio breathing: inhale 4, hold 4, exhale 4, hold 4. Keep every phase comfortable — shorten the count to 3 if 4 feels like a strain. The holds are soft pauses, never a forced breath-hold or bearing down.
movements.regression: Drop the holds entirely and simply breathe in for four and out for four (or out for a little longer). Reduce all counts to three. If any breath-holding feels uneasy, switch to Deep Belly Breathing.
movements.progression: Lengthen each side of the box to a count of five or six once four is effortless, keeping all four phases equal and easy. Stays gentle — longer, slower breathing only, never strained.

contraindications.why: Breath-holds can transiently raise blood pressure and may not suit those with poorly controlled hypertension.
contraindications.substitution: Remove the holds and use a simple long-exhale breath (in for four, out for six) with no pausing.

contraindications.why: Holding the breath can encourage downward bearing or abdominal gripping, which loads a healing pelvic floor and core.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the throat open during the holds, never bear down; if in doubt drop the holds and breathe smoothly.

contraindications.why: For some, holding the breath increases anxiety or air hunger.
contraindications.substitution: Use continuous equal in-and-out breathing with no holds, or move to Deep Belly Breathing.

contraindications.why: Over-controlled breathing occasionally causes light-headedness.
contraindications.substitution: Return to your normal breath, then resume with shorter, gentler counts.

movements.intent: A portable, evenly-paced breath that helps settle a busy nervous system and gather a scattered mind — a pocket of calm available anywhere. Slow, paced breathing supports parasympathetic activation and stress down-regulation, a well-described physiological effect; box breathing is a widely taught form of this [evidence: general slow-breathing autonomic effect is well supported; box-breathing-specific trials are limited — flag].
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: box-breath

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Equal-count breathing with breath-HOLDS (the 'hold for four' phases). This is the one breath move that includes deliberate breath retention. A held breath = a mild Valsalva = transient downward pelvic-floor pressure. For most it is trivial, but for a symptomatic/hypertonic pelvic floor, repeated holds are less ideal than continuous slow breathing. It is restorative and seated, so risk is low, but extended-exhale breathing is the safer default for this cohort.
vetting.evidenceBasis: [evidence: general slow-breathing autonomic effect well supported; box-breathing-specific trials limited — flag.] Clinical reasoning: breath-holds create mild IAP; continuous exhale-led breathing avoids this.
vetting.modificationsRequired: Keep the holds GENTLE and explicitly cue 'let the holds be soft and easy — never strain or clench' (the existing 'nice and easy' helps). Where pelvic-floor symptoms are flagged, prefer extended-exhale-breath as the default close instead.

movements.name: Kind Thoughts Close
movements.dose: 75-120s; 1 round. Scales across all tiers as the final affirmation close; longer in Meditation/No Sweat (up to 120s), 75-90s elsewhere. Tempo: still, slow breaths between affirmations. Rest: this is the close.
movements.setup: Sit or lie however you are most comfortable. Rest one or both hands over your heart or low belly. Let the eyes close or soften toward the floor.
steps: Settle into a comfortable seat or lie down — whatever feels best right now.
Rest your hands over your heart, or one on your heart and one on your belly.
Let your eyes close or your gaze go soft.
Breathe slowly and let each exhale unspool a little tension.
Let the kind words settle in as you hear them.
Take one last full breath, and carry that kindness with you.
veraCues: Hands rest on your heart
Breathe slow and easy
Let the kind words land
You really did this
movements.breath: Slow, natural breathing with a gently extended exhale. No counting, no effort — the breath simply anchors the affirmations. Feel the warmth of your own hands rising and falling.
movements.regression: Lie down fully supported with a cushion under the knees if sitting is tiring. If self-directed kindness feels difficult today, simply rest a hand on the heart and feel the breath — no words needed.
movements.progression: Stay longer and add a brief loving-kindness extension, sending one kind wish to yourself and one to someone you love. Remains entirely restful — depth of reflection, never intensity.

contraindications.why: Hands-on-body stillness and self-directed kindness can stir difficult feelings, including for those with birth trauma or a low postpartum mood.
contraindications.substitution: Rest the hands on the lap instead of the body, keep the eyes open, and treat the affirmations as optional; seek support from a provider if low mood persists.

contraindications.why: Lying flat can niggle a sensitive lower back.
contraindications.substitution: Bend the knees with feet flat, or place a cushion under the knees.

contraindications.why: This is a low-risk rest, but avoid any breath-holding while settling.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the breath flowing freely and natural throughout.

movements.intent: A closing moment to let it land — you showed up for yourself today — offering the same kindness you give everyone else. Self-compassion and brief affirmation practices are associated with improved mood and reduced self-criticism, which matters in the postpartum period [evidence: self-compassion mood link is reasonably supported in psychology literature; magnitude in postpartum specifically is thin — flag]. Never framed as appearance or 'bouncing back'.
movements.posesNote: reuse existing rig: kind-close

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Affirmation/self-compassion close. No physical load. The only thing in my lane is to confirm the copy never drifts into appearance/'bounce-back' framing — the existing text is correctly about showing up, not bodies.
vetting.evidenceBasis: [evidence: self-compassion–mood link reasonably supported in psychology literature; postpartum-specific magnitude thin — flag.]
vetting.modificationsRequired: None. Keep it body-neutral; never reference appearance or weight.

movements.name: Supported Reclined Rest
movements.dose: 60-150s; 1 round. No Sweat / Meditation: 120-150s. Slightly Sweaty: 60-90s. Super Sweaty: 90-150s as the primary down-regulation rest. Tempo: completely still. Rest: this is the rest.
movements.setup: Lie on your back with a rolled towel, cushion, or bolster lengthwise under the spine (or just under the head and knees). Knees can rest bent with feet wide, or supported by a cushion. Arms fall open, palms up.
steps: Place a rolled towel or cushion under your head, and one under your knees.
Lie back and let your whole spine be supported and heavy.
Let your feet drift apart and your knees fall slightly open or stay propped.
Turn your palms up and let your arms be wide and loose.
Close or soften your eyes and let your face go slack.
Stay and breathe naturally; to finish, roll slowly to one side and rise.
veraCues: Let the cushion hold all your weight
Palms up, arms heavy and wide
Let your face go completely slack
There is nowhere to be but here
movements.breath: Effortless natural breathing. Let the body breathe you. If you like, notice the exhale gently lengthening on its own — a quiet cue to the nervous system that the work is done.
movements.regression: No floor required — recline on a sofa or against a stack of pillows at a slight incline with knees supported. Any position where the body is fully held counts.
movements.progression: Lengthen the stay and add a slow head-to-toe softening scan, releasing one body part per exhale. Stays purely restorative — longer and quieter, never more intense.

contraindications.why: Lying flat with straight legs can pull on a sensitive lower back.
contraindications.substitution: Always keep a bolster or cushion under the knees so the lower back rests neutral.

contraindications.why: Extended flat-on-back time may be limited in later pregnancy and uncomfortable for some with high blood pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Recline at a 30-45 degree incline on cushions rather than fully flat.

contraindications.why: Coming up from flat by sitting straight up loads the core like a sit-up.
contraindications.substitution: Always exit by rolling onto your side first and pressing up with the arms — never curl straight up.

contraindications.why: Sudden stillness can let difficult feelings surface for some postpartum.
contraindications.substitution: Keep eyes softly open, keep a hand on the belly to feel the breath, and know it is fine to keep this short.

movements.intent: A fully-supported final rest (a gentle, propped relaxation pose) that lets the body release completely — essential after vigorous Super Sweaty work where deliberate down-regulation matters most. Supported relaxation and slow breathing promote parasympathetic recovery after exertion, aligning with general autonomic-recovery principles and ACOG's emphasis on gentle postpartum progression [evidence: slow-breathing parasympathetic effect well supported; pose-specific trials thin — flag].
movements.posesNote: reuse rig legs-up (supine base, neutral limbs) with limbs eased toward the floor; honest reuse — both are still supine rests, avoids a costly new keyframe rig.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Fully-supported propped relaxation — the primary down-regulation rest after vigorous work. No load. The body-scan progression is purely restful. Safe all tiers; especially valuable after super_sweaty sessions.
vetting.evidenceBasis: [evidence: slow-breathing parasympathetic effect well supported; pose-specific trials thin — flag.] Aligns with ACOG's emphasis on gentle progression/recovery.
vetting.modificationsRequired: None.

movements.name: Gentle Reclined Twist
movements.dose: 40-70s per side; 1 round each side. No Sweat / Meditation: 60-70s/side, deeply slow. Slightly Sweaty: 40-55s/side. Super Sweaty: 50-70s/side as a spine-decompressing closer. Tempo: still hold after a slow entry. Rest: a breath between sides.
movements.setup: Lie on your back and hug both knees gently in. Let both knees drift to one side toward the floor while the shoulders stay heavy and grounded. Arms rest wide in a soft T; turn the gaze away from the knees if the neck likes it.
steps: Lie on your back and draw both knees in toward your chest.
Let both knees lower together to the right, toward the floor.
Keep both shoulder blades heavy on the mat — let the knees stop where the shoulders stay down.
Rest the knees on a cushion if they do not reach the floor.
Breathe into the side ribs and let each exhale soften the twist a little.
Float the knees back to centre, then repeat to the left.
veraCues: Knees drift to one side, slow and soft
Keep both shoulders heavy on the mat
Rest your knees on a cushion if they hover
Turn your gaze away if your neck likes it
movements.breath: Slow nasal breathing into the lengthened side ribs. Let the exhale be the moment the twist deepens, gently and only as far as the shoulders stay grounded. No forcing, no breath-hold, no bearing down.
movements.regression: Keep the knees bent with feet on the floor and lower them only partway, or rest both knees on a stack of cushions so the twist is barely there. Lower one knee at a time if a full twist is too much for the lower back.
movements.progression: Take the top knee a little lower or extend the lower leg long for a deeper rotation, always keeping both shoulders grounded. Remains a gentle, supported twist — never a forced or loaded rotation, in keeping with the postpartum core-safety envelope.

contraindications.why: A twist taken too far or too fast can irritate a sensitive or acute lower back.
contraindications.substitution: Reduce the range, support the knees on cushions, and enter and exit slowly; stop short of any pinch.

contraindications.why: Aggressive trunk rotation can shear across a healing linea alba and tempt abdominal bracing.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the twist small and supported, move with the breath, and avoid any straining or breath-holding; pelvic health physio consensus favours gentle, controlled rotation in early recovery.

contraindications.why: Forcing both shoulders down while the knees pull the other way can strain a stiff or injured shoulder.
contraindications.substitution: Let the top shoulder lift slightly rather than pinning it, or rest that arm on the belly.

contraindications.why: Prolonged flat-on-back time may not suit later pregnancy or some with high blood pressure.
contraindications.substitution: Do a seated version of the twist in a chair instead, turning gently from the waist.

movements.intent: A slow, supported lying twist that decompresses the spine and releases the lower back at the end of a session, restoring gentle rotation lost to all the carrying and feeding. Controlled, low-load spinal rotation is appropriate in postpartum recovery when kept gentle and breath-led, consistent with pelvic health physiotherapy consensus and ACOG's gradual-progression guidance.
movements.posesNote: reuse rig figure-four (supine base, both knees fall to one side, mirrorHalfway true) adapted so both knees lower together; honest reuse of an existing supine side-lying-knees shape, saving a new rig.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Supported supine spinal rotation, slow entry, both shoulders grounded. Gentle, low-load rotation is appropriate postpartum. The deeper-knee progression must keep both shoulders down to avoid forced rotation. Diastasis-neutral, no impact.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Controlled low-load spinal rotation appropriate postpartum when gentle and breath-led (pelvic health physiotherapy consensus; ACOG gradual progression).
vetting.modificationsRequired: Retain 'both shoulders stay grounded, never force the rotation.' Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Neck and Jaw Release
movements.dose: 35-60s per side plus a jaw release; 1 round. No Sweat / Meditation: 50-60s/side. Slightly Sweaty: 35-50s/side. Super Sweaty: 40-60s/side to release clench held during effort. Tempo: slow and still. Rest: a breath between sides.
movements.setup: Sit tall and comfortable, shoulders soft and low. Let one ear drift toward that shoulder for a gentle neck stretch; the opposite hand can rest heavy by your side. Then release the jaw: unclench the teeth, soften the tongue, part the lips slightly.
steps: Sit tall and let both shoulders drop away from your ears.
Let your right ear drift slowly toward your right shoulder.
Let the weight of your head do the stretching — no pulling.
Hold and breathe, then float the head back to centre and switch sides.
Now unclench your jaw: let the teeth part and the tongue rest soft.
Let the lips part slightly and exhale with a soft sigh; repeat the sigh a few times.
veraCues: Let one ear drift toward your shoulder
Let the weight of your head do the work
Unclench your jaw, let the teeth part
Soft sigh out — let the face go slack
movements.breath: Slow breathing with audible soft-sigh exhales through the mouth during the jaw release. The sighing exhale invites the face, jaw, and throat to let go. No pulling on the head and no breath-holding.
movements.regression: Make the neck tilt tiny — even a centimetre of softening counts. Skip the side neck stretch entirely and do only the jaw release and soft sighs if the neck is sensitive.
movements.progression: Add a gentle diagonal — nose toward the armpit to reach the back of the neck — and hold a breath longer, still letting only the head's weight do the work. Stays low-load; never add hand pressure that forces the range.

contraindications.why: Pulling the head with the hand or over-tilting can compress or strain a sensitive cervical spine.
contraindications.substitution: Never use the hand to pull; let only the head's weight stretch, and keep the tilt small. Stop at the first sign of pinching or tingling down the arm.

contraindications.why: Hiking the shoulder up toward the ear cancels the stretch and can strain the trapezius.
contraindications.substitution: Actively let the opposite shoulder slide down and stay heavy; rest that hand in the lap.

contraindications.why: Over-opening the jaw can aggravate a sensitive jaw joint.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the jaw release subtle — just unclench and let the teeth part slightly, without wide opening.

contraindications.why: This is low-risk, but avoid breath-holding during the stretch.
contraindications.substitution: Keep the breath flowing, using the soft-sigh exhale throughout.

movements.intent: A targeted release for the neck and jaw — where the tension of looking down at a little face, and the unconscious clench of a long day, both collect. Releasing habitual jaw and neck tension is a recognised relaxation cue; the soft-sigh exhale supports a parasympathetic shift [evidence: jaw-relaxation-as-stress-release is widely taught but trial evidence is thin — flag; extended-exhale autonomic effect is well supported].
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback; seated micro-movements of the head and jaw are below the avatar's expressive resolution and read better as captions — honestly cheaper than a new rig. Optional later reuse of the seated base if a head-tilt keyframe is ever authored.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Low-load neck/jaw release using only the head's weight — restorative, non-exertional. The source correctly forbids hand pressure that forces range. The diagonal (nose-to-armpit) progression is fine if it stays passive. No BP/impact/core concern.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Relaxation/jaw-release cue with extended-exhale; extended-exhale autonomic effect well supported [evidence: jaw-relaxation-as-stress-release widely taught but trial evidence thin — flag].
vetting.modificationsRequired: Keep 'let only the head's weight do the work — never add hand pressure that forces the range.' Otherwise approved.

movements.name: Long Slow Breath
movements.dose: 75-180s; in for 4, out for 6-8. No Sweat / Meditation: 120-180s and the core breath of the Meditation tier's 'rest' phase. Slightly Sweaty: 75-90s. Super Sweaty: 90-150s to bring breath and heart rate down after vigorous work. Tempo: slow, exhale-led. Rest: this is the rest.
movements.setup: Sit comfortably with a tall, easy spine, or lie supported with knees bent. Rest one hand on the belly and one on the lower ribs so you can feel the breath move. Let the shoulders and face soften.
steps: Settle into a comfortable seat or supported lie-down.
Rest one hand on your belly, one on your lower ribs.
Breathe in slowly through the nose for a count of about four.
Breathe out slowly and fully for a count of six to eight.
Let the exhale empty without forcing, the belly softening as you go.
Keep the breath smooth and unhurried, returning your attention each time it wanders.
veraCues: Breathe in slow, about four
Let the exhale stretch longer, six or eight
Feel your belly soften as you empty
Let the long breath out do the calming
movements.breath: Extended-exhale breathing: inhale roughly 4, exhale roughly 6-8, with no holds. The longer exhale is the active ingredient — it gently nudges the nervous system toward rest. Keep it comfortable; shorten the counts if you feel any air hunger. Never bear down on the exhale.
movements.regression: Make the exhale only slightly longer than the inhale (in 4, out 5) and let the breath be quiet and easy. If counting is distracting, simply make each out-breath a little longer than the one before.
movements.progression: Gradually lengthen the exhale toward a count of eight and stay for longer, optionally adding a soft 'ahh' sigh on the out-breath. Stays gentle — slower and longer breathing only, never strained.

contraindications.why: Forcing or squeezing the exhale can push down on the pelvic floor and core.
contraindications.substitution: Let the exhale flow out passively without abdominal gripping or pushing; pair softly with a natural pelvic-floor lift only if it stays effortless.

contraindications.why: This breath is generally beneficial, but any straining or breath-holding should be avoided.
contraindications.substitution: Keep both phases smooth and continuous with no holding; a long easy exhale is ideal here.

contraindications.why: Lengthening the breath too aggressively can cause light-headedness or a sense of not getting enough air.
contraindications.substitution: Return to normal breathing, then resume with a much smaller difference between inhale and exhale.

contraindications.why: Focusing closely on the breath can heighten anxiety for some.
contraindications.substitution: Open the eyes, soften the focus, and let the breath be background rather than the sole object of attention.

movements.intent: A longer guided breath built around a lengthened exhale — the most reliable, portable way to down-shift after effort and the backbone of the Meditation tier's rest phase. Slow breathing with a prolonged exhale is well documented to increase parasympathetic (vagal) tone and support stress and heart-rate down-regulation; this is among the better-supported claims in the bank, consistent with ACSM/breathing-physiology literature [evidence: solid for the extended-exhale parasympathetic mechanism].
movements.posesNote: caption-only fallback; a seated breath is near-still and already covered by the box-breath/kind-close seated rigs aesthetically — honestly no new rig is warranted. Reuse the seated base statically if any visual is shown.

vetting.contraindicationNotes: Continuous slow breathing with a lengthened exhale and NO breath-holds — the safest breath pattern for this cohort and the best down-regulation tool, with no IAP/pelvic-floor concern. This is exactly the move I would default to over box-breath for any user with pelvic-floor symptoms. The backbone of the Meditation tier.
vetting.evidenceBasis: Slow breathing with prolonged exhale is well documented to raise parasympathetic (vagal) tone and support stress/HR down-regulation — among the better-supported claims in the bank, consistent with breathing-physiology literature. [evidence: solid for the extended-exhale parasympathetic mechanism.]
vetting.modificationsRequired: None. Recommend making this (not box-breath) the default close for any profile flagged with pelvic-floor symptoms.
